entries.name: Canine olfactory cancer detection
entries.one_line: Trained dogs detect cancer by recognizing volatile chemical odors in patient breath and urine samples.
entries.synthesis_md: Trained dogs reliably detect multiple cancer types from breath, urine, and tissue samples, with sensitivity and specificity varying by sample type and training protocol [PMID 25944810]. One dog correctly identified 27 of 30 patients with malignant pulmonary nodules from exhaled gas alone [PMID 31235316], and a single Labrador Retriever detected breast cancer from urine after targeted training [PMID 34200793]. Dogs distinguish invasive melanoma from benign skin lesions in double-blind conditions following extended training periods [PMID 27454583]. The mechanism is a cancer-driven shift in volatile organic compound (VOC) profiles; S-nitrosothiols are elevated in cancer and likely account for the detectable odor signature [PMID 25749837]. Earwax collected from tumor-bearing dogs yields a distinct VOC fingerprint — 18 of 128 volatile metabolites separated cancerous from healthy animals — opening a non-invasive route for detecting cancer in dogs themselves [PMID 37751172].
entries.gap_line: Prospective studies connecting detected cancer VOCs to treatment outcomes and survival prediction remain unexplored.

entries.name: mTOR inhibitors transplant immunosuppression
entries.one_line: Rapamycin-based drugs used to prevent transplant rejection and treat inflammatory conditions, including dosing and side effects.
entries.synthesis_md: Researchers have investigated sirolimus (rapamycin) and related mTOR inhibitors across a wide spectrum of clinical and preclinical contexts, revealing both the breadth of their therapeutic utility and the complexity of their pharmacological management. Studies have examined sirolimus pharmacokinetics in patients with hepatic impairment, finding that reduced clearance warrants dose adjustment in renal transplant recipients. Clinical trials have tested sirolimus against relapsed pediatric leukemia and advanced solid malignancies, exploiting mTOR's role in cell-cycle progression and PI3K/Akt signaling. Investigators have also documented complete responses in autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome and synergistic activity when rapamycin is paired with resveratrol in multiple myeloma cells. Topical sirolimus has emerged as a treatment for vascular lesions such as tufted angioma. Importantly, sirolimus has been shown to directly reactivate latent human polyomaviruses in vitro, raising safety concerns for immunosuppressed transplant patients. Most recently, a randomized controlled trial has begun evaluating once-weekly sirolimus for preserving muscle strength in older adults, extending interest in mTOR inhibition toward healthy aging and longevity.
entries.gap_line: Research halted after 2008; long-term graft survival outcomes and modern real-world transplant effectiveness data post-2010 are entirely absent.

entries.name: Metronomic low-dose chemotherapy antiangiogenic
entries.one_line: Low-dose chemotherapy given continuously to slow tumor growth by cutting off their blood supply.
entries.synthesis_md: Metronomic chemotherapy delivers cytotoxic drugs at low, continuous doses rather than in high-dose pulses, producing antiangiogenic effects that deprive tumors of their blood supply [PMID 25228919]. This scheduling shift reduces toxicity to healthy tissue, enables oral administration, and lowers the risk of acquired drug resistance compared to conventional regimens. Drug repurposing—redirecting approved agents into metronomic protocols—has emerged as a natural complement, expanding the therapeutic toolkit without requiring new drug development. Clinical experience spans breast cancer, head and neck cancer, and pediatric oncology, while preclinical work continues to map the interactions between low-dose scheduling and angiogenic signaling [PMID 27994645]. Exact mechanisms, optimal dosing, and best timing remain incompletely defined, and ancillary studies embedded in ongoing clinical trials are considered the most direct route to resolving them [PMID 25228919].
entries.gap_line: Metronomic chemotherapy combinations with checkpoint inhibitors and cellular immunotherapy remain absent from the literature despite mechanistic synergy potential.

entries.name: G-CSF plerixafor stem cell mobilization
entries.one_line: Using drugs to extract bone marrow stem cells for cancer and blood disorder treatments.
entries.synthesis_md: Autologous stem cell transplantation for multiple myeloma requires collecting enough CD34+ cells to engraft, and the field is actively refining how to mobilize those cells reliably. Daratumumab-based quadruplet induction regimens improve disease response but consistently reduce CD34+ yields and increase reliance on plerixafor rescue [PMID 41860036, PMID 41084543]. Using plerixafor preemptively rather than only as a late rescue improves mobilization success rates, and cost-utility modeling from real-world cohorts supports earlier deployment despite the drug's high cost [PMID 41504007]. Single-dose pegfilgrastim performs comparably to daily filgrastim for steady-state stem cell mobilization [PMID 40281003], while combining cyclophosphamide with G-CSF remains a contested strategy for patients who mobilize poorly [PMID 41651701]. Updated ASCO guidelines help clarify which patients benefit most from colony-stimulating factors across these mobilization contexts [PMID 41740078].
entries.gap_line: Patient response biomarkers and predictive tools for plerixafor mobilization success are absent from the clinical treatment literature.

entries.name: Canine organ allograft survival
entries.one_line: Studies of how to make transplanted organs survive longer in dogs by suppressing immune rejection and pretreating tissue.
entries.synthesis_md: These papers trace the development of allograft survival strategies across organ and islet transplantation, with canine models playing a central role. Early kidney homograft studies in dogs tested antilymphocyte serum and subcellular splenic antigens as immunosuppressive approaches, while clinical liver transplantation series documented gradual improvements in one-year survival across the 1960s and 1970s. Later work extended the field to multivisceral transplantation and ex vivo warm organ perfusion, identifying nitric oxide synthase as a key mediator of vascular integrity during kidney preservation. Ultraviolet light treatment of canine donor islets prolonged allograft survival by dampening immune responses both in vitro and in vivo. Most recently, surface engineering of islets with thrombomodulin and CD47 achieved sustained allogeneic survival without chronic immunosuppression [PMID 41842939], illustrating a broader shift toward localized immunomodulation strategies that simultaneously neutralize innate and adaptive rejection pathways.
entries.gap_line: Mechanistic studies of immunosuppression and allograft rejection in canine models ceased after 1977, creating a 45-year research void before any modern follow-up.

entries.name: Canine cardiac arrhythmia models
entries.one_line: Studies using dogs to understand heart rhythm problems and test drug treatments.
entries.synthesis_md: These papers examine adrenergic and autonomic pharmacology in canine cardiovascular tissues, using isolated vessel preparations and in vivo dog models to characterize receptor function and drug effects. Several studies characterize alpha- and beta-adrenoceptor binding and contractile responses across canine arteries and veins, revealing regional differences in receptor density and subtype distribution. Beta-blockers including bucindolol [PMID 6149305], propranolol, and atenolol are tested for their ability to attenuate adrenergic responses in coronary, femoral, and pulmonary vasculature as well as heart rate. Antiarrhythmic agents such as procainamide and mexiletine are evaluated in dogs with experimentally induced myocardial infarction, where delayed activation potentials serve as electrophysiological endpoints. Additional studies investigate purinergic and adrenergic cotransmission in the canine splenic artery, and isoprostanes are shown to drive vasoconstriction in pulmonary smooth muscle through tyrosine kinase and Rho kinase pathways.
entries.gap_line: The field stalled after 1999 without outcome studies validating whether canine models predict human arrhythmia mechanisms or treatment response.

entries.name: Liposomal muramyl tripeptide immunotherapy
entries.one_line: Immune-stimulating drugs enclosed in fat particles used alongside chemotherapy to fight cancer.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine splenic hemangiosarcoma carries a brutal default prognosis: most dogs die within two months of diagnosis from widespread visceral metastasis, even after splenectomy [PMID 9815908]. L-MTP-PE (liposome-encapsulated muramyl tripeptide phosphatidylethanolamine) targets that biology by delivering an immune-activating signal selectively to pulmonary macrophages and circulating monocytes, turning them into tumor-killing cells [PMID 7900714]. A randomized, multi-institutional trial enrolled dogs with splenic HSA and no gross metastases, stratified them by clinical stage, and tested L-MTP-PE added to splenectomy plus systemic chemotherapy — establishing that macrophage immunotherapy is formally testable in this disease [PMID 9815908]. Confidence in the strategy came partly from osteosarcoma work showing L-MTP-PE achieved a 40% long-term disease-free survival rate in dogs, validating macrophage activation across canine solid tumors [PMID 7900714]. Phase I data confirmed the drug is well tolerated in dogs [PMID 7900714]. This body of work made L-MTP-PE the first immunotherapy evaluated in a randomized setting for canine HSA.
entries.gap_line: Canine hemangiosarcoma clinical efficacy and pharmacokinetic studies remain rare (1% breed_screening focus) despite MTP-PE formulation work spanning 1960s–2000s.

entries.name: JAK-STAT signaling pathway mechanisms
entries.one_line: How cells use JAK and STAT proteins to relay chemical signals that control growth and survival.
entries.synthesis_md: The JAK-STAT pathway links extracellular cytokine signals to changes in gene transcription through a compact chain of events: ligand binding activates receptor-associated Janus kinases, which phosphorylate tyrosine residues on STAT proteins, driving STAT dimerization and nuclear translocation. Specificity across roughly 40 cytokine receptors is encoded partly by SH2 domains within STATs, which determine which family member docks at a given receptor complex. The system is deliberately tunable — phosphatases constrain signal amplitude, and regulatory events at receptors for cytokines such as IL-6 and IL-10 can produce divergent gene-expression outcomes from structurally similar cascades. Oncogenic rewiring, such as the PAX5-JAK2 fusion seen in B-cell leukemia, removes these brakes and drives cytokine-independent signaling. Pathogens exploit the same architecture: a *Yersinia* effector hijacks JAK-STAT to elevate IL-10 and blunt macrophage killing, while JAK inhibitors have shown promise in dampening the cytokine storm driving COVID-associated ARDS. Beyond immunity, JAK-STAT governs developmental timing and tissue-specific proliferation, including beta-cell expansion triggered by prolactin, underscoring how broadly cancer cells and normal physiology alike depend on this pathway.
entries.gap_line: No early-detection studies (0%) and minimal treatment research (5%) in 355 JAK-STAT papers, with clinical translation of these mechanisms completely unaddressed.

entries.name: Galectin-3 canine tumor progression
entries.one_line: How galectin-3 protein organizes epithelial cells and drives breast cancer progression in dogs.
entries.synthesis_md: Galectin-3, a β-galactoside-binding lectin first characterized in dog kidney cells, directs glycoproteins to the apical surface of polarized epithelial cells and maintains normal three-dimensional tissue architecture [PMID 8253805, PMID 16488876]. When galectin-3 is lost, centrosome organization fails and epithelial cells cannot form proper tubular structures [PMID 19923323]. The protein bypasses the classical secretory pathway, exiting cells by unconventional export and re-entering through endosomal recycling, which places it at both intracellular and extracellular sites in the tumor microenvironment [PMID 26059399]. In canine mammary tumors, galectin-1 and galectin-3 expression both shift between benign and malignant tissue, with galectin-1 overexpressed in the majority of malignant cases [PMID 24778023].
entries.gap_line: Therapeutic development and clinical biomarker validation for galectin-3 remain unexplored post-2018 despite decades of mechanistic characterization.

entries.name: PDGFR signaling and activation
entries.one_line: Studies of PDGFR activation in cells and therapeutic approaches to block this growth signal.
entries.synthesis_md: Platelet-derived growth factor receptors (PDGFRs) regulate a wide range of cellular processes, and this collection of papers examines how those receptors are activated, how they signal, and how their dysregulation drives disease. Chromosomal translocations that fuse PDGFR genes to partner sequences such as ETV6 produce constitutively active chimeric kinases that cause atypical myeloproliferative neoplasms including chronic myelomonocytic leukemia. PDGFRα and PDGFRβ form homodimers and heterodimers whose dimer-specific trafficking and downstream signaling dynamics differ meaningfully. In atherosclerotic human arteries, PDGF-C and PDGF-D and their receptors show altered expression, implicating the pathway in vascular disease. PDGFR activation also drives NPM-ALK-positive anaplastic large-cell lymphoma, making the receptor a therapeutic target in that malignancy. Anti-PDGFR antibodies have been linked to scleroderma, and PDGFRβ marks brain pericytes that researchers isolate and characterize using magnetic sorting and immunostaining approaches.
entries.gap_line: PDGFR research has a three-year publication void (2017–2019) with zero papers, followed by sudden recovery in 2020.

entries.name: Tyrosine kinase inhibitor development
entries.one_line: Researchers develop drugs that shut down tyrosine kinases, proteins that drive cancer growth.
entries.synthesis_md: Researchers across multiple fields have investigated tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) as targeted anticancer and anti-inflammatory agents, reflecting the central role these enzymes play in cell growth, division, and survival signaling. Tyrosine kinases, including Tyro3, EGFR, MET, and the erbB family, are frequently overexpressed or dysregulated in tumors of the bladder, lung, and other solid organs, making them compelling drug targets. Medicinal chemists have pursued irreversible inhibitors built on anilinoquinazoline and anilinopyrido-pyrimidine scaffolds, as well as selective small molecules such as SAR125844 and EMD 1204831 targeting the MET receptor. Beyond oncology, TKIs have demonstrated efficacy in dermatological conditions including psoriasis, alopecia areata, and vitiligo. Second-generation TKIs and combination strategies aim to overcome resistance mechanisms that limit first-generation agents, broadening therapeutic options across cancer and inflammatory disease.
entries.gap_line: Research on drug resistance mechanisms stalled post-2020, when publications dropped from 19 to 2 annually despite heavy clinical focus dominating the field.

entries.name: Non-receptor tyrosine kinases
entries.one_line: Enzymes that regulate how cells communicate by chemically marking proteins to activate signals.
entries.synthesis_md: Tyrosine kinases constitute a broad class of enzymes that phosphorylate protein substrates on tyrosine residues and play central roles in cellular signaling. These papers collectively survey the classification, molecular identity, and disease relevance of this enzyme family, covering both receptor and non-receptor members. Individual kinases receive dedicated attention: spleen tyrosine kinase (Syk) is linked to human disease and proposed as a target for small-molecule inhibitor therapy, while Fer, TYRO3, and Abelson tyrosine-protein kinase 2 are characterized as distinct molecular entities with specific expression patterns. Gene amplification of receptor tyrosine kinases and transcriptome-level expression profiling of the broader kinase family highlight how dysregulated tyrosine kinase activity contributes to pathological states. Janus-family kinases receive formal definitional treatment alongside foundational nomenclature entries, grounding the group in standardized biochemical terminology.
entries.gap_line: Post-2020 therapeutic development stalled—no recent work on resistance mechanisms, novel inhibitors, or clinical outcomes for non-receptor tyrosine kinase drugs.

entries.name: Low-dose aspirin cancer prevention
entries.one_line: Studies of whether daily low-dose aspirin can prevent cancer in older adults, particularly colorectal cancer.
entries.synthesis_md: In large human cohort studies, continuous low-dose use (75–150 mg) reliably reduces colorectal cancer incidence over 20 years, though protective effects against non-colorectal cancers remain inconsistent [PMID 37966913]. Network meta-analysis across randomized trials confirms dose-dependent chemopreventive effects for colorectal cancer specifically [PMID 37499126]. Long-term low-dose aspirin also reduces primary cancer risk in cardiovascular disease survivors [PMID 36612095]. The critical tradeoff is bleeding: low-dose aspirin cuts major vascular events by approximately 11% but raises major bleeding risk by 40–50%, so net benefit depends on individual risk ratios [PMID 40010936]. Platelet function testing reveals substantial individual variability in aspirin response, and that variability associates with clinical outcomes [PMID 40040304].
entries.gap_line: Aspirin cancer prevention research focuses almost entirely on colorectal cancer; other major cancer types like breast, lung, and prostate remain unstudied.

entries.name: PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway targeting
entries.one_line: Developing cancer drugs that shut down cellular growth pathways to stop tumors from multiplying.
entries.synthesis_md: The PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis sits at the center of tumor cell survival, growth, and resistance to therapy across a wide range of cancer types [PMID 34916492]. When this pathway is aberrantly activated, it suppresses tumor suppressors like TSC1/TSC2 and drives unchecked proliferation, metastasis, and resistance to both chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Researchers have developed several inhibitor classes to disrupt it — including rapalogs that dissociate TORC1, dual TORC1/TORC2 allosteric inhibitors, and catalytic PI3K/mTOR blockers — yet single-agent trials have consistently produced modest response rates, suggesting that redundant feedback loops blunt efficacy. Upstream activators like NEIL3 and downstream effectors remain active targets for combination strategies. The recurring finding across tumor types is that durable control likely requires hitting multiple nodes of the pathway simultaneously rather than any single component [PMID 34916492].
entries.gap_line: HSA-specific PI3K/AKT/mTOR research is nearly absent (1% breed screening track), leaving canine inhibitor resistance mechanisms unstudied.

entries.name: Canine behavior and communication
entries.one_line: Research on dog behavior, training, breeding, and how dogs have shaped human culture.
entries.synthesis_md: These papers examine dogs as a cultural, linguistic, and symbolic presence across American and Western humanistic traditions rather than as biological subjects. Several studies trace the contested authorship and racial politics embedded in the song "Hound Dog," from Big Mama Thornton's original blues recording through Elvis Presley's televised performances and the song's downstream influence on figures such as John Lennon and Bruce Springsteen. A separate strand addresses canine cognition and narration in Jack London's fiction, arguing that Buck in Call of the Wild reflects Darwinian thinking about interspecies relations rather than simple allegory. Legal scholarship interrogates whether dogs qualify as property under larceny law. Etymological analysis pursues the origins of phrases such as "lie doggo." Together the papers treat the dog as a site where questions of race, authorship, law, language, and literary representation converge.
entries.gap_line: The 2020-2022 period shows near-zero primary research on canine communication mechanisms despite pandemic-driven pet adoption surges.

entries.name: SBRT hepatocellular carcinoma management
entries.one_line: Stereotactic body radiation therapy for liver cancer, which may be combined with drugs or other techniques to improve outcomes.
entries.synthesis_md: Recent studies in stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) highlight both technical innovations and expanding clinical indications across multiple disease sites. For prostate cancer, simulation-free workflows on MR-Linac using diagnostic reference scans have demonstrated dosimetric feasibility, potentially streamlining patient preparation [PMID 41932402], while the CYGNUS multicenter study showed that salvage SBRT remains viable for pelvic nodal oligorecurrent disease even within previously irradiated territories [PMID 41864432]. Dosimetric analysis from the LUNAR trial further characterized absorbed dose distributions in oligorecurrent hormone-sensitive prostate cancer [PMID 41927282]. In thoracic oncology, deep inspiration breath-hold reduced irradiation of organs at risk compared to free-breathing lung SBRT [PMID 41924679], and SBRT successfully salvaged a late NSCLC staple-site recurrence confirmed by endobronchial ultrasound [PMID 41881483]. Updated ESTRO-EORTC guidelines standardized target delineation for stage I–III small cell lung cancer chemoradiotherapy [PMID 41932500]. For pancreatic tumors, CyberKnife tracking errors had modest effects on organ-at-risk doses, though patients with large target-to-OAR proximity required closer monitoring for inter-fraction deformation [PMID 41936944].
entries.gap_line: Comparative effectiveness versus hepatic resection and transplant remains unexplored despite rapid treatment literature growth.

entries.name: Cutaneous angiosarcoma treatment outcomes
entries.one_line: Research on treating angiosarcoma, a rare skin cancer from blood vessels, with new immunotherapy drugs.
entries.synthesis_md: Cutaneous angiosarcoma of the head and neck carries more immunotherapy-response biomarkers than visceral primaries, and genomic sequencing of 143 cases confirms that primary site drives distinct tumor biology [PMID 34638300]. The DART trial provided the first prospective evidence for dual checkpoint blockade—ipilimumab plus nivolumab—in metastatic or unresectable angiosarcoma, moving the field beyond isolated case reports [PMID 34380663]. A seven-patient case series documented early real-world experience with checkpoint inhibitors in this disease [PMID 31395100]. B-cell density in the tumor microenvironment predicts survival and immunotherapy response across sarcoma subtypes. A phase 2 study combined the IDO1 inhibitor epacadostat with pembrolizumab in a vascular sarcoma cohort to test whether augmenting immune activation could improve on pembrolizumab's modest single-agent activity [PMID 36971773]. Anatomic site of origin is the dominant stratification variable—a principle directly applicable to subtype-aware trial design in canine hemangiosarcoma.
entries.gap_line: Molecular mechanisms and prognostic biomarkers predicting treatment response are absent from a literature of 1438 papers dominated by surgical case reports.

entries.name: Canine mitral valve disease
entries.one_line: Research on mitral valve disease in dogs, using ultrasound imaging to diagnose and assess heart function.
entries.synthesis_md: Myxomatous mitral valve disease (MMVD) is the most prevalent acquired cardiac condition in dogs, accounting for nearly half of canine cardiac referrals in retrospective analyses [PMID 15481588], and the ACVIM consensus panel has formalized diagnostic and treatment recommendations that stratify patients by disease stage before and after onset of clinical signs [PMID 30974015]. Echocardiographic measurement of left atrial size is central to that staging, yet studies reveal meaningful variability in how veterinary echocardiographers select images, place calipers, and define enlargement thresholds [PMID 40819468], while volume estimates derived from different imaging planes are statistically similar but not interchangeable [PMID 36801487]. Cavalier King Charles Spaniels have left atrial-to-aortic ratios no smaller than those of other breeds, so generic reference intervals remain applicable to this breed [PMID 40373502]. In preclinical ACVIM B1 dogs without cardiomegaly, pimobendan significantly reduced post-exercise lactate at day 180 and a greater proportion of owners in the treatment group reported improved activity compared with the placebo group [PMID 31581204]. Cardiac biomarkers such as natriuretic peptides complement imaging by helping differentiate cardiac from non-cardiac causes of respiratory distress [PMID 26040814].
entries.gap_line: Treatment efficacy and surgical outcomes are understudied despite early detection research driving 56% of publications.

entries.name: Canine Trypanosoma and Leishmania infections
entries.one_line: Parasitic infections in dogs and humans like Chagas disease and leishmaniasis, covering diagnosis, treatment, and disease patterns.
entries.synthesis_md: Chagas disease and canine leishmaniasis share landscapes of limited treatment options, complex transmission routes, and difficult vector surveillance. Treatment for Chagas disease currently relies on just two outdated drugs, and combining benznidazole with difluoromethylornithine has shown promise for boosting trypanocidal efficacy [PMID 41982456]. Oral transmission through food contaminated with infected triatomines triggered an acute outbreak investigation in semi-arid Bahia, Brazil [PMID 41457259], underscoring the diversity of exposure pathways beyond vector bites. Autonomous kissing bug kill traps tested across Texas, Guatemala, and Mexico captured over 1,500 adult triatomines across multiple prototype iterations [PMID 41758873], improving prospects for standardized vector surveillance. In Brazil, dogs serve as the primary reservoir of Leishmania infantum, and species-level epidemiological work in Rio de Janeiro reveals diverse Leishmania species circulating in canine populations [PMID 41813617]. Sand fly vectors also carry arboviruses, and a newly developed RT-PCR assay detected Pacuvirus coinfection in dogs with visceral leishmaniasis [PMID 41605293], revealing a previously underexplored layer of pathogen co-circulation in canine reservoir hosts.
entries.gap_line: Therapeutic intervention studies represent just 5% despite diagnostic papers exploding since 2025, suggesting drug development and vaccine research remain critically underdeveloped.

entries.name: Natural antivirals influenza inhibition
entries.one_line: Research on plant-based compounds that could prevent or fight influenza infections.
entries.synthesis_md: Plant-derived compounds inhibit influenza replication through several distinct mechanisms. Flavonoids from *Scutellaria baicalensis* suppress viral replication in MDCK cells by 50% at 20 μM and 90% at 40 μM, acting during the intracellular replication phase rather than at attachment [PMID 7742801, PMID 7741518]. A flavonoid-enriched extract from the same root reduces acute lung injury in influenza-infected mice in vivo [PMID 30668313]. Cirsimaritin, a flavonoid from *Artemisia scoparia*, blocks replication by downregulating NF-κB signaling [PMID 29783993], while volatile oils including patchouli alcohol and eucalyptol suppress influenza pneumonia through TLR4/NF-κB/NLRP3 pathways [PMID 40757669]. Aloe-emodin reduces cytopathic effect and viral replication concentration-dependently via galectin-3 upregulation [PMID 24877694]. Chemically modifying EGCG with long-chain fatty acids amplifies its anti-influenza activity 24-fold over the native compound [PMID 18547804]. Multi-herb oral formulas attenuate viral replication 10- to 100-fold in animal models, but their components interact pharmacokinetically with oseltamivir [PMID 23588232].
entries.gap_line: Clinical efficacy and in vivo studies are missing—field stalled at MDCK cell screening with only 1% treatment track.

entries.name: Vinyl chloride carcinogenicity rats
entries.one_line: Vinyl chloride's mechanisms of causing cancer in rats through DNA damage, p53 mutations, and angiosarcoma formation.
entries.synthesis_md: Hemangiosarcoma begins in hepatic endothelial cells because those cells repair DNA damage far less efficiently than surrounding hepatocytes [PMID 7432520]. Vinyl chloride produces liver angiosarcomas in both rodents and humans through the same organotropic mechanism, making it a cross-species model for this tumor type [PMID 15989139]. The carcinogen works by forming etheno (ε) adducts—particularly N2,3-ethenoguanine—that reach steady-state concentrations in target tissues [PMID 10626206]. Critically, identical etheno adducts arise endogenously from lipid peroxidation in unexposed animals and humans, revealing a spontaneous mutagenic route relevant to sporadic disease [PMID 10767618]. Arsenic separately drives liver angiosarcomas in humans by blocking DNA repair rather than directly damaging DNA [PMID 7463514]. O6-methylguanine accumulates preferentially in nonparenchymal target cells, explaining why endothelium—not hepatocytes—becomes the tumor-of-origin [PMID 7093953, 7432520].
entries.gap_line: Prevention and treatment of VC-induced angiosarcoma have never been explored in the rat literature.

entries.name: Canine elastography ablation monitoring
entries.one_line: Ultrasound elastography measures tissue stiffness in dogs for disease diagnosis and monitoring ablation effects.
entries.synthesis_md: Elastography reliably maps tissue stiffness across canine abdominal organs—liver, spleen, kidneys, and prostate—with excellent reproducibility when technical variables are controlled [PMID 25619362, PMID 32974311]. High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) ablation stiffens canine liver tissue in proportion to deposited energy, and elastograms depict these lesions as harder than surrounding tissue with strain contrasts between −2.5 and −3.5 dB [PMID 10574342]. Spatial-angular compounding further improves strain image contrast-to-noise ratio, sharpening ablation zone boundaries [PMID 15831331]. Sonoelastography distinguishes malignant from benign testicular lesions in dogs, confirming that tissue stiffness differences carry real diagnostic weight in spontaneous canine tumors [PMID 25286658]. Anesthesia state, respiratory phase, and scanning approach all significantly alter liver stiffness readings, so standardized acquisition protocols are essential before elastography can reliably monitor ablation responses in clinical oncology [PMID 35155659].
entries.gap_line: Post-ablation treatment monitoring is critically underexplored, with 96% of papers addressing early detection rather than tracking efficacy after tissue ablation.

entries.name: Thymoquinone cancer synergy mechanisms
entries.one_line: Research into how thymoquinone, a compound from nigella sativa, boosts cancer drug effectiveness by triggering cancer cell death.
entries.synthesis_md: Thymoquinone (TQ), derived from Nigella sativa black seed, attacks tumors through multiple overlapping mechanisms: apoptosis induction, cell cycle arrest, autophagy activation, and blockade of PI3K/AKT/mTOR, STAT3, and Wnt/β-catenin signaling [PMID 40045027]. Combined with 5-fluorouracil or imatinib, TQ overcomes drug resistance and boosts tumor cell killing in colorectal cancer models [PMID 41353854, PMID 40143604]. TQ sensitizes lung cancer cells to cisplatin and radiation by amplifying apoptosis [PMID 39946226]. In breast cancer, TQ lowers CD55 expression to reduce immune evasion and stabilizes G-quadruplex structures in oncogene promoters to suppress proliferation [PMID 41893940]. Poor aqueous solubility limits TQ's bioavailability, but nanoparticle encapsulation restores effective intracellular delivery [PMID 41416651]. Across cancer types, TQ amplifies existing treatments — chemotherapy, radiation, and immunotherapy — rather than acting as a standalone agent [PMID 41303508].
entries.gap_line: In vivo animal models and human clinical data are entirely absent despite explosive growth in cell-culture studies since 2024.

entries.name: VEGFR signaling and angiogenesis
entries.one_line: The molecular signals that make blood vessels grow in tumors and how to block them for cancer therapy.
entries.synthesis_md: Tumors depend on new blood vessels to sustain growth, and the VEGF/VEGFR axis drives that process [PMID 37169756]. VEGFR-2 activity is tuned by post-translational modifications: lysine methylation promotes receptor activation, while c-Cbl-mediated ubiquitination suppresses PLCγ1 phosphorylation and brakes the angiogenic switch. Neuropilin-1 and neuropilin-2 act as essential VEGFR coreceptors—co-depleting both blocks tumor angiogenesis and triggers VEGF receptor degradation. Therapeutic strategies include cyclic peptides modeled on the VEGF-B α1 helix that bind VEGFR-1 and VEGFR-2 and curb tumor growth in murine models, alongside multi-target kinase inhibitors like lenvatinib that simultaneously suppress VEGFR, FGFR, and RET. Collectively, these studies chart multiple leverage points—receptor post-translational control, coreceptor scaffolding, and broad kinase blockade—where the tumor angiogenic program can be interrupted.
entries.gap_line: Early detection biomarkers are nearly absent from this literature despite their clinical importance for HSA prognosis.

entries.name: Sulforaphane isothiocyanate anticancer activity
entries.one_line: Sulforaphane from broccoli stops cancer cells from growing by triggering cell death and immune responses.
entries.synthesis_md: Sulforaphane (SFN), an isothiocyanate concentrated in broccoli and other cruciferous vegetables, kills endothelial cells through multiple mechanisms — a property directly relevant to hemangiosarcoma, a tumor of endothelial origin. SFN activates the pro-apoptotic protein Bax in endothelial progenitor cells, driving them into programmed cell death [PMID 19034579]. It also arrests endothelial cells at the G2/M checkpoint and disrupts microtubule polymerization, blocking the cell division that sustains vascular tumors [PMID 16938492]. SFN modulates Nrf2 in two directions — activating cytoprotective genes in normal cells while destabilizing cancer cells that have hijacked constitutive Nrf2 activity for survival [PMID 22583415]. Epidemiological data link higher cruciferous vegetable intake to reduced risk of multiple cancers [PMID 30079608].
entries.gap_line: Human clinical trials demonstrating anticancer efficacy are absent despite 118 papers documenting sulforaphane's supplement bioactivity in lab models.

entries.name: Canine hemangiosarcoma therapeutic targets
entries.one_line: Scientists study cancer in dogs to improve diagnosis and treatment for both pets and people.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine hemangiosarcoma (HSA) arises from blood vessel endothelial cells and kills most dogs within months of diagnosis despite chemotherapy. Genomic sequencing confirms that canine HSA and human angiosarcoma share key driver mutations, making dogs a critically needed model for developing new therapies [PMID 31570656]. Molecular subtyping of HSA uncovers distinct tumor subtypes that parallel those in human angiosarcoma, enabling biology-driven treatment selection. Tumor biomarkers support earlier diagnosis, prognosis prediction, and real-time monitoring of treatment response — capabilities actively reshaping personalized cancer care [PMID 38763973]. The PD-L1/PD-1 axis drives immunosuppression inside the tumor microenvironment, and PD-L1 is broadly overexpressed across solid tumors, making it an actionable immunotherapy target [PMID 35317524]. Metronomic chemotherapy — continuous low-dose administration rather than maximum tolerated dosing — reduces toxicity and resistance and is gaining ground in veterinary oncology.
entries.gap_line: Immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors, CAR-T cells) remains completely untracked despite transforming human cancer treatment.

entries.name: Withaferin anticancer mechanisms
entries.one_line: Withaferin compounds from ashwagandha and how they work to kill cancer cells.
entries.synthesis_md: Withaferin A (WA), a steroidal lactone from *Withania somnifera*, targets tumor growth through converging pathways with direct relevance to hemangiosarcoma: it suppresses angiogenesis, triggers apoptosis, and reduces inflammation [PMID 37076466]. Combined with radiation in gastric cancer models, WA amplified tumor kill by blocking autophagy and disrupting mitochondrial function [PMID 40467837]. *Withania somnifera* compounds also inhibit KAT6A, an epigenetic enzyme driving cancer cell proliferation and metastasis, with active constituents competing at its acetyl-CoA binding site [PMID 40120183]. *Withania somnifera* has been evaluated as a cytoprotective co-treatment against cyclophosphamide-induced organ damage — a question directly relevant to standard HSA chemotherapy regimens [PMID 38853132]. Withanolide composition determines extract potency and pharmacology, making standardization essential before clinical translation [PMID 37808919]. Preclinical safety data are broadly favorable, though rare reversible liver toxicity has been reported [PMID 40968393].
entries.gap_line: Human clinical trials for withaferin anticancer activity are absent; the field consists entirely of preclinical studies and traditional medicine literature.

entries.name: Hericium erinaceus bioactive compounds
entries.one_line: How compounds from lion's mane mushrooms could fight cancer and boost brain and immune function.
entries.synthesis_md: Lion's Mane mushroom (*Hericium erinaceus*) produces mycelium-derived erinacines and glucan polysaccharides that kill cancer cells through separate but complementary mechanisms. Erinacine A suppresses tumor cell growth and invasion across multiple human cancer models by disrupting key oncologic signaling pathways [PMID 36736282, PMID 28854418]. A distinct erinacine triggers cancer cell death by opening the mitochondrial permeability transition pore through inhibition of the PI3K/Akt/GSK-3β signaling axis [PMID 30355923]. Purified H. erinaceus polysaccharides independently induce apoptosis in precancerous cells and carry the highest free-radical-scavenging activity among eight medicinal mushroom species tested [PMID 29431066, PMID 25954912]. None of these experiments involve dogs or vascular sarcomas; all findings originate from human cell lines and human tissue specimens, leaving direct relevance to canine hemangiosarcoma untested.
entries.gap_line: Clinical efficacy trials and mechanistic studies in neurological disease models remain absent despite decades of extraction and characterization work.

entries.name: Salmonella antimicrobial resistance genetics
entries.one_line: How Salmonella bacteria in food and water develop resistance to antibiotics and ways to combat this threat.
entries.synthesis_md: Research on Salmonella prevention and control spans vaccine innovation, phage biocontrol, resistance surveillance, and rapid detection. Vaccine efforts include engineering S. Enteritidis mutants to overproduce outer membrane vesicles as immunogenic candidates [PMID 41955656] and constructing a programmed lysis system coupled with self-assembled nanoparticles for controlled antigen delivery [PMID 41998728]. Lytic phages MM-1 and MM-2 show activity against both enterohemorrhagic E. coli and Salmonella, offering antibiotic-free biocontrol for fresh-produce safety [PMID 41990421]. Antimicrobial resistance surveillance reveals serious food-chain risks: raw beef in Ethiopia harbors Salmonella, Shigella, and E. coli carrying superbug-level resistance profiles [PMID 41945625], and whole-genome sequencing of clinical Gram-negative isolates in Ghana has uncovered high-risk AMR clones requiring continent-wide monitoring [PMID 41990102]. Detection technology has advanced as well, with a phage-functionalized hydrogel biosensing platform combining bioluminescence and SERS enabling rapid simultaneous quantification of live and dead Salmonella Typhimurium on food surfaces [PMID 41996810].
entries.gap_line: Molecular mechanisms driving resistance—specific genes and mutations—are absent; papers characterize resistant strains without explaining their genetic basis.

entries.name: Fucoidan anticancer mechanisms
entries.one_line: A compound from brown algae is being tested as a potential cancer treatment.
entries.synthesis_md: Fucoidan, a sulfated polysaccharide from brown seaweed, engages multiple anticancer pathways, though its exact mechanisms vary by species, molecular weight, and growth conditions [PMID 30621045]. Brown algae fucoidans are anti-angiogenic — a property directly relevant to hemangiosarcoma, which arises from dysregulated vascular endothelial cells [PMID 37175646]. Low-molecular-weight fucoidan (LMWF) reduces myeloid-derived suppressor cell activity and sensitizes tumors to immune checkpoint blockade [PMID 41570786]. LMWF also amplifies chemotherapy-induced cell death in drug-resistant cancer cells, reducing viability beyond what chemotherapy achieves alone [PMID 36967501, 34360807]. Fucoidan binds P-selectin with nanomolar affinity, and P-selectin is upregulated on activated endothelial cells — the cell type that gives rise to hemangiosarcoma [PMID 39562382]. These overlapping mechanisms position fucoidan as a biologically plausible adjunct in HSA treatment protocols.
entries.gap_line: Post-2019 decline from 12 to 5 papers by 2022 indicates mechanistic research plateaued without visible progression to pharmaceutical formulation strategies or clinical validation studies.

entries.name: Tumor treating fields glioblastoma
entries.one_line: Electric field therapy for newly diagnosed glioblastoma (brain cancer), tested alone and with standard chemotherapy drugs.
entries.synthesis_md: Glioblastoma kills most patients within 12–15 months despite surgery, temozolomide, and radiation, driven by extreme tumor heterogeneity and clonal plasticity [PMID 35582442]. Tumor Treating Fields (TTFields) — alternating electric fields at 100–300 kHz — add a physical mechanism to that arsenal by disrupting dividing cancer cells [PMID 41259185]. Skin heating from Joule effects caps deliverable field strength, but pulsed-delivery protocols restore efficacy while limiting thermal injury [PMID 41259185]. Patients who use the device ≥75% of the time during the first three months achieve better progression-free survival than lower-compliance patients [PMID 41473753]. Post-marketing surveillance of more than 25,000 CNS-malignancy patients confirms an acceptable safety profile [PMID 38949692]. Preclinical and simulation work now extends TTFields optimization to breast, colorectal, and pancreatic cancers, broadening the mechanistic case for alternating-field therapy across solid tumors [PMID 41760592, 40387458].
entries.gap_line: Predictive biomarkers for TTFields response and mechanisms of acquired resistance remain largely unexplored.

entries.name: Circulating tumor DNA biomarkers
entries.one_line: Blood tests that detect cancer by finding tumor DNA fragments, enabling earlier diagnosis without traditional biopsies.
entries.synthesis_md: Liquid biopsy captures tumor-derived material — circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), circulating tumor cells, and extracellular vesicles — from blood and other body fluids without requiring tissue sampling. Technologies such as droplet digital PCR, BEAMing, and tagged-amplicon deep sequencing make it possible to detect and quantify these fragments at low concentrations. ctDNA methylation patterns have drawn particular attention because they offer high specificity across cancer types and can flag disease earlier than other markers, though sensitivity and standardization remain active problems [PMID 39525954]. In metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, ctDNA fraction tracks closely with disease burden measured by both serum markers and imaging, and higher ctDNA levels associate with worse treatment outcomes [PMID 38418825]. Serial sampling during immunotherapy can reveal clonal shifts in real time, giving clinicians a dynamic view of how tumors evolve under treatment pressure.
entries.gap_line: Treatment monitoring and post-therapy recurrence prediction are substantially understudied compared to early detection's 67% dominance across the 213 papers.

entries.name: Oncolytic virotherapy immunotherapy combinations
entries.one_line: Combining engineered viruses that attack cancer with immune-activating drugs to improve tumor destruction.
entries.synthesis_md: Oncolytic viruses kill tumors through direct cell lysis and immune activation, making them natural partners for checkpoint inhibitor combinations [PMID 38309616]. The plant virus CPMV, delivered as an in situ vaccine, reprograms the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment toward an immune-activated state and has demonstrated anti-tumor activity in canine cancer patients [PMID 34575893]. CPMV also upregulates checkpoint regulators on effector T cells, providing a direct biological rationale for co-administration of anti-PD-1 agents [PMID 34575893]. Engineered oncolytic viruses now carry immunostimulatory payloads — IL-12, p53 — to convert immunologically cold tumors hot [PMID 41768263, 41721933]. Tumor COX-2 overexpression drives prostaglandin E2 production that suppresses T-cell activity, and COX inhibitors combined with checkpoint blockade target this immune evasion mechanism [PMID 33020239]. Oncolytic reovirus selectively replicates in Ras-activated tumor cells and adds immunomodulatory effects independent of direct lysis [PMID 41963749].
entries.gap_line: The field produced 16 papers from 1986-2023, then surged to 62 in 2024-2026, yet lacks mechanistic studies of combination synergy.

entries.name: Drug repurposing AI applications
entries.one_line: Finding new disease treatments by applying AI to identify untapped uses of existing approved drugs.
entries.synthesis_md: Drug repurposing finds new therapeutic uses for approved compounds, compressing development timelines and costs compared to de novo drug discovery. Repurposed candidates arrive with established safety profiles, pharmacokinetic data, and manufacturing processes already characterized [PMID 32807237]. Computation accelerates discovery: network-based systems pharmacology integrates disease biology with drug-target interaction data to rank candidates without exhaustive bench work [PMID 32194980]. Deep learning models trained on large biomedical datasets surface non-obvious compound-disease connections at scale. Graph neural network tools like TxGNN identify candidates even for diseases with no existing approved therapies. Repurposing also yields molecules with novel mechanisms of action—important when resistance limits the effectiveness of standard agents [PMID 37652975]. The workflow proceeds from computational nomination to preclinical validation to Phase II trials [PMID 36852389]. For hemangiosarcoma, where dedicated pharmaceutical investment is scarce, AI-driven repurposing is the most actionable near-term route to new treatment options.
entries.gap_line: Repurposing for cancer, neurology, and rare disease remains unexplored despite explosive growth in COVID-driven applications since 2020.

entries.name: Doxorubicin delivery chemotherapy systems
entries.one_line: Research on nanoparticle systems to deliver doxorubicin chemotherapy to cancer cells and overcome drug resistance.
entries.synthesis_md: Doxorubicin remains a cornerstone chemotherapeutic agent studied across a wide range of delivery strategies, resistance mechanisms, and combination regimens. Researchers have explored liposomal and biodegradable polymer platforms to achieve controlled, sustained local release that limits systemic toxicity while maintaining antitumor efficacy. Nanoparticle formulations, including polyethylenimine nanogels loaded with ultrasmall iron oxide and doxorubicin, integrate MRI-guided imaging with chemotherapy to improve targeted tumor delivery. Studies on drug resistance in MCF-7 breast cancer and colon carcinoma cells have probed how doxorubicin modulates telomerase activity, apoptotic gene expression, and DNA damage pathways. Prodrug nanoassemblies pairing doxorubicin with GPX4 inhibitors exploit ferroptosis synergistically for triple-negative breast cancer. Concurrent doxorubicin with external beam radiotherapy has been evaluated clinically for bulky differentiated thyroid carcinoma [PMID 30128032].
entries.gap_line: Clinical Phase II/III trials and patient-derived efficacy data are entirely absent, explaining the research collapse after 1998 when early nanoparticle delivery moved out of favor.

entries.name: Nanoparticle drug delivery systems
entries.one_line: Designing tiny particles that carry cancer drugs directly to tumors while protecting healthy cells.
entries.synthesis_md: Conventional chemotherapy for canine hemangiosarcoma carries the same liabilities these papers document across oncology: poor bioavailability, systemic toxicity, non-specific targeting, and drug resistance. Nanoparticle platforms — liposomes, polymeric nanoparticles, dendrimers, and metal-organic frameworks — protect drugs from early elimination and control tumor-site release, reducing the collateral damage of high-dose regimens [PMID 36746307, PMID 35970275]. Smart nanoparticles that respond to biological cues deliver therapeutics precisely while sparing healthy tissue [PMID 37919282]. Engineering these platforms to navigate systemic, microenvironmental, and cellular barriers accounts for the patient-to-patient heterogeneity that undermines fixed-dose protocols [PMID 33277608]. Functional ligands targeting receptors overexpressed on malignant cells improve selective intracellular uptake without systemic collateral damage. Long-acting parenteral formulations using PLGA microspheres and in-situ depots reduce daily-dosing burden and support adherence across extended treatment courses [PMID 37160247].
entries.gap_line: Diagnostic nanoparticles for HSA detection are completely unexplored; research focuses exclusively on therapeutic drug delivery systems.

entries.name: Flavonoid efflux transporter inhibition
entries.one_line: How plant flavonoids block cellular pumps that normally remove drugs from the body.
entries.synthesis_md: Flavonoids block multiple drug efflux transporters, raising circulating levels of co-administered drugs. Quercetin significantly increased peak plasma concentration and area under the curve for clopidogrel in both rats and dogs [PMID 23139136]. Of 35 flavonoids screened in MRP2-overexpressing cells, 8 were confirmed MRP2 substrates, with chemical structure governing transporter affinity [PMID 31006447]. Flavonoids also inhibit SLC46A3, a lysosomal transporter that limits nanoparticle uptake in tumor cells — blocking it enhances intratumoral drug delivery [PMID 39579794]. Tetrahydropalmatine enantiomers selectively inhibit P-glycoprotein while sparing MRP1 and BCRP, demonstrating that plant-derived compounds override efflux with specificity [PMID 22900779]. Berberine moves through hepatocytes via OCT1 uptake and MDR1 P-gp efflux in a vectorial process that models active drug expulsion from cells [PMID 18157518]. These transporter nodes are targets for improving chemotherapy retention in canine tumors.
entries.gap_line: No clinical trials or disease-outcome studies exist; research is confined to in vitro MDCK and microsomal models despite two decades of work.

entries.name: Ginsenoside pharmacokinetics canine plasma
entries.one_line: How ginseng compounds move through and build up in dog bodies during safety and effectiveness studies.
entries.synthesis_md: Ginsenoside Rh2, explicitly labeled an anticancer nutrient derived from ginseng, reaches roughly 16% oral bioavailability in dogs — far higher than the 5% measured in rats — and concentrates in liver and gastrointestinal tissues after oral dosing, both primary HSA sites [PMID 19524010]. Validated LC/MS/MS methods detect Rg3 in dog plasma at concentrations as low as 0.5 ng/mL, providing the analytical infrastructure needed to track these compounds in clinical trials [PMID 15714599]. A 26-week oral toxicity study of Rg3 in beagles establishes a preliminary safety baseline for extended dosing schedules [PMID 31726191]. Six months of repeated intravenous administration reveals potential accumulation of protopanaxadiol-type ginsenosides, flagging a monitoring requirement for sustained treatment protocols [PMID 27840091]. Disease state further shifts pharmacokinetics: diabetic animals show substantially higher ginsenoside Rb1 systemic exposure through increased intestinal permeability and impaired gut deglycosylation, signaling that the metabolic environment of tumor-bearing dogs may demand individualized dosing adjustments [PMID 26265741].
entries.gap_line: The field measures plasma ginsenoside levels exclusively without paired pharmacodynamic studies demonstrating therapeutic efficacy in canine models.

entries.name: Pomegranate peel bioactive extraction
entries.one_line: Extracting healing compounds from pomegranate peel to create medicines and food preservation products.
entries.synthesis_md: Pomegranate peel extract (PPE) delivers hydrolysable ellagitannins that suppress cancer cell proliferation in vitro across multiple tumor types [PMID 41226652, PMID 38628214]. The extract reduces COX-2, TNF-α, and IL-6 in treated cancer cell lines—inflammatory mediators that drive vascular tumor growth [PMID 40777945]. Against oral squamous cell carcinoma, PPE shows measurable, dose-dependent cytotoxicity with a quantifiable IC50 [PMID 38107049]. Oral bioavailability has historically limited translation, but nano-phytosomal encapsulation with phosphatidylcholine now achieves sustained gastrointestinal release while preserving antioxidant activity [PMID 38040148]. In a rat model, PPE nanoparticles protected gonadal tissue from cyclophosphamide-induced damage, confirming in vivo tolerability at biologically active doses [PMID 41559827]. Together, these findings position pomegranate peel bioactives as a deliverable, tolerable adjunct candidate for inflammation-driven vascular cancers like hemangiosarcoma.
entries.gap_line: Human clinical trials validating therapeutic efficacy are nearly absent—only 5% treatment papers versus 94% supplement formulation studies—leaving antioxidant compounds therapeutically unvalidated in humans.

entries.name: Global burden disease epidemiology
entries.one_line: Research examining the global burden of disease and how different healthcare systems respond to it.
entries.synthesis_md: These papers apply predictive modeling, simulation, and evidence synthesis to improve clinical and health system decision-making. Studies develop individualized IVF models estimating cumulative live birth probability over multiple treatment cycles [PMID 35866894] and quantify the impact of COVID-19-related delays on fertility outcomes across age and infertility subgroups [PMID 33226080]. A system dynamics tool helps planners test emergency care interventions for older patients [PMID 36702512]. Researchers in Lebanon document the challenges of health economic costing during simultaneous financial, pandemic, and humanitarian crises [PMID 36287378]. Saudi Arabia-based work proposes a clinical practice guideline appraisal framework aligned with national health reform goals [PMID 37450240]. A separate review identifies knowledge gaps around gene expression profile testing as a prognostic tool in cutaneous melanoma [PMID 32725204]. Together, these studies advance structured, quantitative approaches to clinical prediction and health systems planning.
entries.gap_line: Early detection outcome validation lags far behind screening burden research (9% vs 58%), leaving the evidence chain between detection and clinical benefit undeveloped.

entries.name: Pentacyclic triterpene anticancer agents
entries.one_line: Plant molecules being researched as potential new cancer drugs based on their ability to stop cancer cells.
entries.synthesis_md: Pentacyclic triterpenes sourced from birch bark and frankincense resin kill cancer cells through multiple overlapping mechanisms. Betulin and betulinic acid — both abundant in birch bark — trigger apoptosis and suppress tumor proliferation across cancer types, with preclinical and early clinical evidence supporting both chemopreventive and chemotherapeutic roles [PMID 39723067]. Semisynthetic betulinic acid derivatives conjugated with indole groups show stronger cytotoxicity against melanoma cells than the parent compound [PMID 38202344], and novel amide derivatives of betulonic acid demonstrate selective activity against breast cancer and melanoma lines in vitro [PMID 38674101]. Boswellic acids from frankincense resin produced measurable anti-proliferative effects in a Phase Ia window-of-opportunity trial in breast cancer patients [PMID 38194131]. Betulin also reduces intestinal toxicity from 5-fluorouracil while preserving its antitumor effect, pointing toward a role in combination chemotherapy regimens [PMID 40840122].
entries.gap_line: Bioavailability and pharmacokinetic characterization of these compounds remain absent from a field dominated by in vitro screening, despite historical poor absorption being a known barrier for pentacyclic triterpenes.

entries.name: microRNA biomarkers cancer
entries.one_line: Finding small RNA molecules that reveal what type of cancer someone has and how they'll respond to treatment.
entries.synthesis_md: MicroRNAs function as both diagnostic biomarkers and functional regulators across a broad spectrum of cancers and neurodegenerative conditions. In lung squamous cell carcinoma, the hsa-miR-520a/MYO19 axis modulates ferroptosis and immune evasion, linking miRNA-mediated gene silencing to tumor immune escape. In melanoma, hsa-let-7c-5p, hsa-miR-130b-3p, and hsa-miR-142-3p distinguish benign, primary, and metastatic disease stages through multi-dataset transcriptomic screening. Tumor-suppressive miRNAs have been identified across additional malignancies — hsa-miR-154 targets the circadian gene CLOCK in breast cancer, hsa-miR-323a-3p suppresses STAT3 in neuroblastoma, and miR-140-5p influences MDM2 expression in p53-dependent gastric cancer subtypes. CircRNA-mediated sponging of miR-326 and miR-503-5p drives osteosarcoma progression through VDAC1/UBE4A regulation. Beyond oncology, plasma levels of hsa-miR-200a-3p, hsa-miR-502-3p, hsa-let-7d-5p, and hsa-let-7g-5p show diagnostic utility for Alzheimer's disease, broadening the translational relevance of miRNA profiling from tumor biology to neurodegeneration.
entries.gap_line: Prospective early-detection screening studies testing miRNA panels in asymptomatic populations remain absent.

entries.name: PROTAC targeted protein degradation
entries.one_line: Designed molecules that link disease-causing proteins to cellular destruction machinery for therapy.
entries.synthesis_md: PROTACs hijack the cell's own ubiquitin-proteasome system to physically destroy cancer-driving proteins rather than merely inhibiting them, giving this approach an edge in tumors with resistant or structurally undruggable targets [PMID 36415148]. Oncoproteins like MYC — intrinsically disordered and lacking a clean binding pocket — become tractable when a bifunctional molecule recruits an E3 ligase to mark them for destruction [PMID 41900110]. Molecular glue degraders extend the same logic: halofuginone eliminates integrin β4, a protein whose overexpression fuels aggressive phenotypes across solid tumors [PMID 41874446]. Newer platforms engage both proteasomal and lysosomal pathways, broadening the degradable proteome beyond cytoplasmic targets [PMID 41874277]. Self-assembled nano-PROTACs directly address poor solubility and membrane permeability — the main barriers blocking clinical translation of conventional bifunctional degraders [PMID 41932497]. For canine hemangiosarcoma, a vascular tumor with few approved targeted therapies and a tendency toward rapid drug resistance, this degradation-first chemical strategy represents a genuinely new therapeutic direction worth tracking.
entries.gap_line: Early detection represents just 1% of PROTAC research, leaving diagnostic applications almost entirely underdeveloped.

entries.name: Tea polyphenols cancer prevention
entries.one_line: Understanding how tea compounds could prevent cancer by affecting how genes are turned on and off.
entries.synthesis_md: Plant polyphenols—including quercetin, green tea EGCG, hawthorn flavonoids, and astragalus constituents—suppress cancer cell proliferation and block tumor angiogenesis across multiple cancer types [PMID 37232542, PMID 41217940]. Quercetin performs with greater in vitro potency against metastatic cancer cells than against localized disease cells [PMID 37232542]. EGCG from green tea preserves gap junction intercellular communication in Mardin-Darby canine kidney cells exposed to a renal carcinogen, demonstrating direct chemopreventive action in a canine epithelial model [PMID 15736408]. Network pharmacology analyses of hawthorn and astragalus compounds identify convergent cancer-relevant molecular targets that span overlapping signaling networks [PMID 38327605, PMID 36976504]. Quercetin's established anti-angiogenic and cytotoxic properties make it a high-priority compound to evaluate against hemangiosarcoma, a tumor of vascular endothelial origin that requires pathological vessel formation to grow [PMID 41217940].
entries.gap_line: Human clinical trials for cancer prevention are absent—the field is 90% mechanistic supplement studies with no real-world efficacy evidence in people.

entries.name: Cannabinoid veterinary immunology
entries.one_line: How cannabis compounds like CBD and THC affect immune function and inflammation in pets and people.
entries.synthesis_md: Research into cannabinoid pharmacology increasingly centers on the interplay between THC and CBD, and on the distinct roles of CB1 and CB2 receptors in mediating physiological and pathological responses. CBD dose-dependently modulates THC-induced motor activity, hypothermia, and antinociception in ways that differ by genetic background and sex, underscoring that THC:CBD ratios—rather than single-compound doses—determine behavioral outcomes [PMID 41959067]. This ratio-dependent logic extends to neuroprotection: specific CBD:THC combinations outperform CBD-only hemp extracts in limiting neuropathological damage after traumatic brain injury [PMID 41997410]. At the receptor level, endocannabinoids display dynamic subtype selectivity between CB1 and CB2 [PMID 41962866], and CB1 biased signaling has emerged as a plausible therapeutic target for schizophrenia-related symptoms given high cannabis co-use in that population [PMID 41992094]. CB2 agonism is also under investigation for inflammatory disease, with aryl-cyclohexanone demonstrating in vitro and in silico anti-inflammatory activity through the endocannabinoid immunomodulatory pathway [PMID 41937628]. Clinically, chronic heavy cannabis use carries liabilities including cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome, affecting roughly one-third of regular users [PMID 41923507].
entries.gap_line: No studies document how cannabinoids affect specific immune measures—cytokines, T-cell phenotypes, antibody responses—despite 77 papers in the field.

entries.name: Metformin diabetes cancer prevention
entries.one_line: Metformin and diabetes drugs being studied for cancer prevention alongside treating obesity and heart disease.
entries.synthesis_md: Metformin activates AMPK, directly disrupting the metabolic pathways cancer cells depend on for survival and proliferation [PMID 40738017]. A meta-analysis of 37 studies covering more than 1.4 million participants finds that metformin reduces colorectal cancer incidence with a measurable dose-response relationship [PMID 41347173]. Chronic hyperinsulinemia, systemic inflammation, and metabolic dysregulation together create tumor-promoting environments, and antidiabetic interventions targeting these factors offer cancer prevention [PMID 40701453]. Preclinical data show that metformin combined with fasting-induced hypoglycemia activates the PP2A-GSK3β-Mcl1 axis and inhibits tumor growth, motivating an ongoing presurgical breast cancer trial [PMID 41165048]. In a mouse colorectal cancer model, metformin protects intestinal mucosal barrier integrity and reduces cancer onset and progression [PMID 40969386]. Low-dose metformin alone fails to reduce polyp burden in a genetic cancer model, showing that dose and combination strategy determine efficacy [PMID 41362256].
entries.gap_line: Mechanistic studies of metformin's direct anti-cancer activity are absent, with 90% of research examining preventive associations in diabetics.

entries.name: Epigenetic memory and inheritance systems
entries.one_line: How cells and organisms age through inherited chemical modifications to DNA that control gene activity.
entries.synthesis_md: These papers examine how epigenetic mechanisms encode, transmit, and lose biological information across cells, generations, and disease states. DNA methylation, histone modification, and chromatin remodeling regulate aging by eroding the epigenetic landscape over time, and DNAm-based clocks can capture both lifespan and healthspan [PMID 36336680]. Acquired DNA methylation at CpG islands passes from parents to offspring in mice, demonstrating that environmentally induced epigenetic changes are heritable [PMID 36754048]. Epigenetic programs also shape innate immune memory after infection, set the pace of human neuronal maturation, and control macrophage polarization during wound repair. Disrupted epigenetic states contribute to major depression and to autophagy dysregulation. Restoring youthful epigenetic information through cellular reprogramming produces functional recovery in aging tissues, underscoring broad therapeutic potential across these systems.
entries.gap_line: Transgenerational epigenetic inheritance mechanisms are vastly underexplored despite being central to the neighborhood's scope; single-organism pathway research dominates instead.

entries.name: TME immune checkpoint resistance mechanisms
entries.one_line: How the tumor microenvironment shields cancer from immune checkpoint drugs.
entries.synthesis_md: The tumor microenvironment in hemangiosarcoma is an active driver of disease, not a passive scaffold [PMID 36917948]. Cancer-associated fibroblasts remodel the extracellular matrix, fuel angiogenesis, and suppress immune infiltration through direct crosstalk with immune cell populations [PMID 34635121]. This coordinated suppression produces what researchers call a "cold" tumor — one where immune effectors are physically excluded and immune checkpoint blockade fails to generate a response [PMID 39420203]. Checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1 and PD-L1 have shown clinical benefit in select solid tumors, but only a minority of patients respond, and the resistance mechanisms are deeply embedded in TME architecture [PMID 36807772]. Spatial profiling technologies now map exactly where these suppressive cell-cell interactions occur within the TME, giving researchers a structural blueprint for where combination therapies must intervene to convert cold tumors into immunologically active ones [PMID 36800999].
entries.gap_line: Breed-specific genetic drivers of checkpoint resistance are vastly underrepresented in mechanistic research relative to species-agnostic treatment studies.

entries.name: Indoleamine dioxygenase gut microbiota
entries.one_line: Gut bacteria break down tryptophan and create compounds that control which of your genes are turned on or off.
entries.synthesis_md: The papers collectively examine tryptophan catabolism through the kynurenine pathway — particularly indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO) activity — as a mediator of immune dysregulation in chronic disease. Elevated IDO activity and kynurenine accumulation link metabolic syndrome to inflammation and oxidative stress [PMID 41732501], while artemether and related natural products suppress kynurenine production in triple-negative breast cancer as an alternative to direct IDO1 inhibition [PMID 41823303]. A single exercise bout shifts tryptophan-kynurenine metabolism and NK cell function in heart failure patients [PMID 41668769]. Gut microbiota taxa including Burkholderiales and Eubacteriaceae causally influence colorectal cancer risk through plasma metabolites [PMID 41997092], and multi-omics analyses identify metabolic-immune regulatory genes central to ulcerative colitis [PMID 41972123, 41959722]. Epigenetic reprogramming, mitochondrial dysfunction, and programmed cell death emerge as shared mechanisms threading through these inflammatory contexts.
entries.gap_line: Early detection biomarkers for IDO-related dysbiosis represent just 1% of research while therapeutic approaches claim 74%.

entries.name: Hypoxia-HIF angiogenesis pathways
entries.one_line: How cells sense oxygen deprivation and grow new blood vessels in inflammatory diseases like arthritis, endometriosis and PCOS.
entries.synthesis_md: These papers address chronic inflammatory and hormone-related diseases along two main threads: targeted drug delivery for joint diseases and epigenetic dysregulation in female reproductive disorders. Osteoarthritis research examines intra-articular delivery systems — nanoparticles, microspheres, and hydrogels — engineered to overcome rapid drug clearance in the joint cavity [PMID 31524006; PMID 35547865]. Rheumatoid arthritis studies describe biomimetic nanoparticles co-delivering anti-inflammatory cytokines and dexamethasone to restore macrophage balance [PMID 35363114], while network pharmacology identifies luteolin from Siegesbeckia orientalis as an active therapeutic compound [PMID 37390879]. Researchers frame endometriosis as a genetically heterogeneous, epigenetically driven condition [PMID 30527836], and separate work shows polycystic ovary syndrome transmits transgenerationally through DNA hypomethylation. One study also demonstrates that curcumin suppresses tumor growth by destabilizing the HIF-1 transcription complex through ARNT degradation [PMID 16880289].
entries.gap_line: Human clinical trials for curcumin and other supplements targeting hypoxia-angiogenesis remain entirely absent despite 73% of preclinical work on supplement interventions.

entries.name: Canine transmissible venereal sarcoma
entries.one_line: Cancer in dogs spread during mating, studied for tumor biology, immune response, and drug resistance.
entries.synthesis_md: CTVT is the world's oldest continuously propagating cancer clone, transmitting itself as a living parasitic allograft dog-to-dog via sexual contact for thousands of years [PMID 26232412]. The tumor cell itself is the infectious agent, capable of crossing major histocompatibility barriers [PMID 23981098]. It suppresses MHC antigen expression during progressive growth, then loses that ability as the host immune system reasserts control and the tumor regresses spontaneously [PMID 2949770]. Transcriptome analysis identifies 459 differentially expressed genes between progressive and regressing tumors, illuminating the molecular switch behind this reversal [PMID 23237908]. Vincristine reliably induces remission, though recurrence can appear at unusual sites — including the uterine stump of spayed dogs — after apparent cure [PMID 38952767]. In non-endemic countries like the UK, virtually all cases arrive via imported dogs [PMID 34773267].
entries.gap_line: Diagnostic strategies and early detection methods are nearly absent from the literature despite clinical importance for this transmissible tumor.

entries.name: Canine tumor-associated coagulopathy
entries.one_line: Dogs with cancer often develop dangerous blood clotting disorders and platelet problems.
entries.synthesis_md: Hemangiosarcoma is a coagulation-disrupting cancer. Ten percent of dogs with cancer develop thrombocytopenia, with sarcomas contributing meaningfully to that burden [PMID 7884725]. Dogs with hemangiosarcoma show measurable dysfunction on standard coagulation panels, and those abnormalities worsen as disease type and stage advance [PMID 22280938]. Dogs presenting with spontaneous hemoperitoneum—HSA's most common acute crisis—carry concurrent coagulopathy and hyperfibrinolysis that track directly with shock severity as measured by plasma lactate [PMID 26220266]. Sarcoma-bearing dogs have a detectable prevalence of thromboembolism, with TEG and D-dimer providing the most predictive hemostatic readouts [PMID 37548637]. Dietary n-3 fatty acid supplementation was evaluated specifically in HSA dogs to preserve platelet function and limit coagulation dysregulation [PMID 10587259]. Coagulopathy in HSA is not a complication—it is a defining feature of the disease.
entries.gap_line: Treatment protocols and breed-specific genetic risk factors remain completely unexplored, with the literature restricted to diagnostic early detection.

entries.name: Canine gonadectomy health outcomes
entries.one_line: Studies on how neutering and spaying affect dogs' cancer risk, aging, behavior, and overall health across different breeds.
entries.synthesis_md: Removing the gonads before sexual maturity consistently raises a dog's lifetime cancer risk, and the earlier the surgery, the higher the hazard [PMID 24432963, PMID 40628281]. Gonadal steroids anchor cancer-protective machinery that evolution has calibrated to each species' reproductive strategy and lifespan [PMID 35993010]. Strip those hormones away and multiple tumor lineages increase: breed-level data show elevated rates of mast cell tumors, lymphoma, and other malignancies in gonadectomized dogs [PMID 24432963]. The brain is not spared; oestrogen deficiency after neuter associates with accelerated brain aging and elevated tumor risk [PMID 31467332]. A recent systematic review also questions whether pre-pubertal spaying reliably prevents mammary tumors — the classical justification for early surgery [PMID 39943206]. Neuter status is therefore a bona fide oncological risk variable, not merely a reproductive management decision [PMID 31799281].
entries.gap_line: Treatment and intervention strategies for post-gonadectomy health complications are nearly absent, with 94% of research focused on identifying breed-specific risks rather than mitigating them.

entries.name: Canine ocular hemangiosarcoma
entries.one_line: Eye cancer in dogs, particularly blood vessel tumors on or around the eyelid.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine ocular HSA arises most often on the conjunctiva and third eyelid, where hemorrhage is a common presenting sign. Surgical excision is the primary treatment, and histopathology is required to confirm the diagnosis [PMID 34402571]. A 52-dog retrospective series documented recurrence rates and long-term outcomes after conjunctival hemangioma or HSA resection [PMID 34402571]. UV exposure is implicated in corneal vascular tumor pathogenesis in dogs, and these tumors remain exceptionally rare, with most reports still at case-series level [PMID 41893675]. Diagnosing malignant spindle-cell variants from the nictitating membrane is genuinely difficult; only three nonpigmented cases were recorded across 23 years in Scandinavia, and immunohistochemistry alone may not resolve the tumor type [PMID 38396026]. When disease progression requires enucleation, ophthalmology-service procedures produce lower complication rates than emergency-service enucleations [PMID 41985523].
entries.gap_line: Early detection frameworks are severely underexplored at 4% of papers, despite constituting the natural clinical follow-up to the extensive breed-screening work that dominates the literature.

entries.name: Toceranib canine osteosarcoma treatment
entries.one_line: Testing targeted cancer drugs to treat bone cancer in dogs.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine osteosarcoma survival rates have not improved in four decades, creating urgent demand for novel drug combinations [PMID 36012610]. Sorafenib, a multi-kinase inhibitor, acts synergistically with doxorubicin in both human and canine osteosarcoma cell lines [PMID 36012610]. YM155, a survivin inhibitor, potentiates etoposide's cytotoxicity against canine osteosarcoma in vitro and in vivo [PMID 31308291]. Toceranib phosphate demonstrates primary antitumor activity in canine nasal carcinoma as both a standalone and adjuvant agent [PMID 34655275], and produces partial tumor reduction in KIT/PDGFR-positive intranasal sarcoma when paired with firocoxib. Calcium phosphate-coated lipid nanoparticles co-delivering doxorubicin and curcumin counteract P-glycoprotein-driven multidrug resistance in osteosarcoma cell lines [PMID 39566701]. Together, these studies position multi-kinase inhibition and targeted drug delivery as the leading combination strategies for drug-resistant canine solid tumors.
entries.gap_line: Toceranib resistance mechanisms and clinical predictors of treatment response remain absent despite treatment studies comprising 64% of papers.

entries.name: Canine hemangiosarcoma adjuvant therapy
entries.one_line: Improving survival in dogs with aggressive hemangiosarcoma through chemotherapy and immunotherapy.
entries.synthesis_md: Splenectomy followed by doxorubicin-based chemotherapy is the standard of care for splenic hemangiosarcoma, but prognosis remains poor even with treatment [PMID 26225611, PMID 30197439]. Carboplatin has been evaluated as an alternative to doxorubicin after splenectomy, and delivering adjuvant chemotherapy promptly after surgery further improves outcomes in non-metastatic disease. For stage III metastatic disease, doxorubicin is routinely administered despite a recognized absence of supporting outcome data, prompting direct comparison of maximum-tolerated-dose, metronomic, and no-treatment strategies [PMID 31251441]. Investigators are moving beyond cytotoxic drugs: an autologous whole-cell vaccine showed preliminary anti-tumor activity with fewer adverse events than anthracyclines [PMID 33208160], dendritic cell vaccination combined with low-dose doxorubicin has entered clinical evaluation, and the mushroom-derived polysaccharopeptide I'm-Yunity was prospectively tested alone and alongside doxorubicin post-splenectomy [PMID 35442554].
entries.gap_line: Genetic predisposition and early-detection biomarkers are unexplored despite literature's exclusive focus on post-diagnosis doxorubicin chemotherapy.
