entries.name: Canine osteosarcoma chemotherapy
entries.one_line: Chemotherapy approaches for bone cancer in dogs, often combined with surgery or radiation.
entries.synthesis_md: Carboplatin delivers limited benefit against macroscopic canine hemangiosarcoma: in a retrospective series of 28 dogs with mesenchymal tumors — including 12 with HSA — the overall response rate across the whole cohort was 14.2%, confirming that palliative chemotherapy options for gross disease remain scarce [PMID 37705417]. Doxorubicin, the other cornerstone of HSA protocols, harms more than the left ventricle — a prospective study enrolling dogs with multiple cancer types, including hemangiosarcoma, found right ventricular function declined measurably with repeated doses. Dietary omega-3 fatty acid supplementation did not alter doxorubicin pharmacokinetics in dogs with lymphoma receiving the drug — no significant differences in serum concentrations or clearance were detected between high and low omega-3 diet groups [PMID 16426224]. Combining molecular iodine with doxorubicin reduces treatment-related adverse events without diminishing tumor response in canine cancer patients [PMID 29530037].
entries.gap_line: Immunotherapy and checkpoint inhibitor studies are entirely absent, leaving canine osteosarcoma treatment anchored to doxorubicin protocols from the 1970s while human oncology has pivoted to precision medicine.

entries.name: Canine circulating cell-free DNA biomarkers
entries.one_line: Using DNA circulating in dog blood to detect and diagnose cancers earlier.
entries.synthesis_md: cfDNA is detectable in canine plasma across cancer, sepsis, and trauma [PMID 28295988]. Whole-genome sequencing of plasma cfDNA from hemangiosarcoma-affected dogs reveals distinct fragment size profiles and copy number alterations compared to healthy dogs, and machine learning classifiers built on these features enable early detection [PMID 39872607]. Dogs with benign tumors produce cfDNA fragment patterns nearly identical to healthy controls; malignant tumors generate clearly aberrant signals [PMID 39272211]. ctDNA analysis in dogs with splenic lesions is feasible and helps distinguish hemangiosarcoma from benign masses before splenectomy [PMID 35428782]. Circulating nucleosomes — histone-wrapped DNA fragments released by dying tumor cells — are also elevated in HSA-affected dogs, offering an ELISA-based complement to sequencing approaches. Together, these tools are building a non-invasive diagnostic pipeline for a cancer that currently requires surgery to confirm.
entries.gap_line: Cross-breed cfDNA variation and breed-specific biomarker development remain unexplored, despite dogs' remarkable genetic diversity affecting disease susceptibility.

entries.name: Canine splenic imaging diagnosis
entries.one_line: Using ultrasound and CT imaging to diagnose and characterize spleen diseases in dogs.
entries.synthesis_md: Standard ultrasound identifies splenic masses but cannot reliably separate malignant from benign. Contrast-enhanced ultrasound extends vascular characterization to splenic and hepatic nodules, improving lesion differentiation over grayscale alone [PMID 29530040]. Splenic extramedullary hematopoiesis appears as hypervascular nodules on multiphase CT, directly mimicking hemangiosarcoma and confounding noninvasive triage [PMID 32579754]. In dogs with hemoperitoneum from splenic rupture, preoperative liver nodules on ultrasound are more often benign than metastatic — meaning staging ultrasound overstates disease spread before surgery [PMID 39111340]. Rare tumors like splenic stromal sarcoma share overlapping ultrasound features with hemangiosarcoma, hematoma, and lymphoid hyperplasia, compounding the diagnostic challenge [PMID 39520713]. CT texture analysis captures tissue heterogeneity invisible to the human eye and is a candidate biomarker for distinguishing hemangiosarcoma from nodular hyperplasia. CT and ultrasound produce complementary rather than redundant findings in dogs with confirmed hepatic and splenic lymphoma [PMID 34352128].
entries.gap_line: Treatment outcome studies following splenic imaging diagnosis remain virtually absent, with only 4% of papers addressing management versus 73% detection.

entries.name: Canine cancer nutritional support
entries.one_line: How diet and supplements can support dogs with cancer, including weight management and reducing inflammation.
entries.synthesis_md: Nutraceutical and nutritional interventions are gaining ground in canine cancer supportive care. In a double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial, 100 cancer-bearing dogs on chemotherapy received oligo fucoidan from brown seaweed or placebo and were monitored by CBC and serum biochemistry every two to three weeks for three months [PMID 34864255]. Fish oil targets the cytokine-driven body composition changes common in dogs with serious systemic illness [PMID 9857337]. Liver-protective agents—S-adenosylmethionine, N-acetylcysteine, ursodeoxycholic acid, silymarin, and vitamin E—provide cytoprotection in canine hepatobiliary disease directly relevant to chemotherapy-stressed livers [PMID 19524797]. Spirulina carries established hypolipidemic and antioxidant activity across animal species [PMID 39592737], and phytoextract blends significantly reduce C-reactive protein in older dogs [PMID 41394907]. These approaches give HSA patients practical tools for reducing oxidative burden and systemic inflammation alongside standard treatment.
entries.gap_line: Nutritional support integration with active cancer treatment (chemotherapy, radiation) remains nearly absent despite being how most treated dogs receive care.

entries.name: Canine hemangiosarcoma genomic profiling
entries.one_line: Researchers study a deadly blood vessel cancer in dogs to identify genetic targets for new treatments and understand human cancer.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine hemangiosarcoma and human angiosarcoma share a mutational landscape, making dogs a genomically valid — and practically scalable — model for a cancer too rare in humans to power large sequencing studies [PMID 31570656]. Exome sequencing of canine HSA cases first identified actionable driver mutations, including recurrent PIK3CA alterations, that mirror findings in human angiosarcoma [PMID 29190660]. The PI3K-alpha inhibitor alpelisib shows in vitro anticancer activity against PIK3CA-mutated canine HSA cell lines, directly translating that genomic finding into a drug target. Targeted sequencing of 109 splenic HSA cases links specific genomic alterations to clinical features including metastasis at diagnosis and overall outcome [PMID 37734854]. RNA sequencing across both species identifies novel protein-coding gene fusions that activate convergent angiogenic transcriptional programs. Substantial genomic heterogeneity within individual tumors and between patients complicates the use of any single mutation as a stand-alone biomarker.
entries.gap_line: Pre-symptomatic screening biomarkers underexplored—only 7% of papers develop early-detection tools despite genomic profiling's core clinical application in risk stratification.

entries.name: Canine endocrine tumor disorders
entries.one_line: Studies of tumors and hormone disorders in dogs' endocrine glands like the thyroid, adrenal and pituitary.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine endocrine tumors span multiple organ systems but share a common thread: hormone excess drives the clinical picture before the mass itself becomes the primary problem. Anal sac adenocarcinomas cause humoral hypercalcemia—calcium rises, hypercalciuria follows, and both resolve when the tumor is removed [PMID 6300551]; a nude-mouse transplant line confirmed the tumor itself secretes the responsible circulating factor [PMID 3012199]. Pancreatic islet cells generate distinct syndromes: glucagonoma presents with superficial necrolytic dermatitis and hypoaminoacidemia [PMID 11023126], and concurrent gastrinoma and somatostatinoma can arise in a single dog [PMID 15053935]. GH-secreting mammary tumors drive acromegaly in intact females, and tumor removal normalizes GH and IGF-I levels [PMID 22214858]. Hypercalcemia—arising through parathyroid, adrenal, thyroid, and malignancy-related pathways—emerges as the most consequential systemic complication across this entire tumor class [PMID 23415381].
entries.gap_line: Molecular mechanisms and genetic drivers remain uncharacterized; research focuses on clinical presentation and screening without addressing underlying tumor biology.

entries.name: Canine models human cancer research
entries.one_line: Dogs with naturally occurring cancers help researchers develop new treatments for humans and learn how body bacteria affects tumor growth.
entries.synthesis_md: Dogs share cancer biology with humans at the genomic, transcriptomic, and clinical levels, making them the most informative spontaneous model for studying malignancies across the spectrum—including hemangiosarcoma [PMID 35595353]. Next-generation sequencing of the canine genome enables direct comparison of tumor-driving mutations, including in DNA damage response genes such as BRCA1, BRCA2, and p53, across species. Carcinogens produce tumors at anatomically matched sites in both species, validating cross-species extrapolation of findings [PMID 31795923]. Complex phenotypes in dogs—such as extreme intestinal polyposis—recapitulate human familial cancer syndromes at the molecular level [PMID 30018743], and dysbiosis in dogs with intestinal tumors mirrors the microbiome disruption documented in human patients [PMID 29852000, PMID 38927960]. Precision medicine, built on individual tumor molecular profiling, is the organizing framework now driving bidirectional discovery between veterinary and human oncology [PMID 35595353].
entries.gap_line: Therapeutic validation studies using canine models to test cancer drugs (treatment 10%) are nearly absent compared to biological characterization work (supplements 29%), limiting translational pathways to human trials.

entries.name: Canine pericardial effusion management
entries.one_line: Managing fluid buildup around dogs' hearts through diagnosis, drainage, and surgery.
entries.synthesis_md: Pericardial effusion in dogs most often signals neoplasia or idiopathic pericarditis, and telling them apart determines prognosis [PMID 12831077]. Hemangiosarcoma of the right atrium and chemodectoma of the heartbase are the two most common cardiac tumors [PMID 12831077]. Echocardiography is the first-line imaging tool; cytologic analysis of the effusion can confirm lymphoma directly from the fluid [PMID 16279390], but overall cytologic yield for identifying specific tumor type is limited [PMID 24236526]. Pericardial fluid pH does not reliably distinguish neoplastic from idiopathic effusions; data from the two groups overlap in 89% of cases and median pH values are not significantly different, providing little justification for its diagnostic use [PMID 12892303]. For dogs without an identifiable cause on preoperative echo, thoracoscopic pericardectomy combined with pericardioscopy resolves tamponade and allows direct visual assessment of the pericardial space [PMID 30516842]. Accurate tumor identification at this stage determines whether intervention is palliative or potentially curative.
entries.gap_line: Treatment efficacy and long-term prognosis after pericardiocentesis or pericardiectomy are virtually absent despite heavy diagnostic focus.

entries.name: Veterinary cancer therapy approaches
entries.one_line: Different approaches to treating cancer in animals, from radiation and medicine to integrative therapies.
entries.synthesis_md: Veterinary oncology is rapidly expanding beyond conventional surgery and chemotherapy. New pharmaceutical agents—including species-specific monoclonal antibodies approved in 2021—are entering clinical practice for companion animals [PMID 35790168]. Translating human immunotherapy tools such as checkpoint inhibitors and tumor-antigen vaccines faces biological compatibility hurdles, but targeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitors and novel chemotherapeutics like rabacfosadine show promise across species [PMID 41702788]. Gut dysbiosis is now recognized as a driver of chemoresistance, and probiotic interventions are being explored to restore immune responsiveness during cancer treatment [PMID 39954194]. Complementary modalities—including acupuncture, oxygen therapies, and phototherapies—are increasingly paired with conventional care to support quality of life [PMID 40883158]. Together, these developments describe a field actively diversifying its treatment options while working to match the pace of human oncology.
entries.gap_line: Immunotherapy and precision molecular therapies are completely absent from papers on veterinary cancer treatment.

entries.name: Canine PD-L1 immunotherapy models
entries.one_line: Developing cancer immunotherapy using dog tumor models to test PD-L1 targeting approaches for both animals and humans.
entries.synthesis_md: Dogs naturally develop sarcomas in immunocompetent hosts that closely mirror human disease, making them a superior translational platform for testing immunotherapy [PMID 37377896]. Human anti-PD-L1 monotherapies achieve durable responses in only 20–40% of patients, and purpose-built caninized anti-PD-L1 and anti-CTLA-4 antibodies now give researchers matched tools to probe why combination approaches perform better [PMID 37377896]. Collagen-anchored IL-2 and IL-12 safely reprogram the tumor microenvironment in spontaneous canine soft tissue sarcomas without triggering the off-tumor toxicity that has historically capped cytokine dosing [PMID 37014656]. CAR T cells targeting B7-H3, overexpressed on canine sarcomas, demonstrate target killing in vitro and extend an FDA-approved hematologic technology into the solid-tumor setting [PMID 30963322]. These converging tools position the dog as an active testbed for sequencing checkpoint blockade, cytokine therapy, and adoptive cell transfer against sarcomas including HSA.
entries.gap_line: Canine PD-L1 resistance mechanisms and adaptive immune evasion remain unexplored, absent from top terms despite being critical for understanding therapeutic durability.

entries.name: canine sarcoma signaling pathways
entries.one_line: Laboratory research on dog cancer to understand how tumor cells develop and what makes them grow.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine splenic hemangiosarcoma expresses JAK1, JAK2, and STAT3 in their phosphorylated, activated forms, placing the JAK-STAT signaling axis at the center of HSA biology [PMID 31958674]. Paclitaxel delivered as Paccal Vet kills two HSA cell lines in a dose- and time-dependent fashion, with IC50 values in the clinically achievable range of 7–610 ng/mL, and shifts VEGF and bFGF expression in the surviving cells [PMID 30267450]. Closely related canine sarcomas — particularly histiocytic sarcoma — run on MAPK pathway mutations in PTPN11, KRAS, and BRAF that constitutively activate ERK and AKT, establishing receptor tyrosine kinase cascades as a shared oncogenic engine across the canine sarcoma family [PMID 32212266, PMID 37231193]. Survivin, an apoptosis-blocking protein highly expressed in canine sarcoma cell lines, represents an additional druggable node: inhibiting it reduces proliferation and increases cell death [PMID 24260303]. This convergent signaling landscape gives HSA researchers a prioritized map of targets already validated in neighboring tumors.
entries.gap_line: Early-detection research comprises only 4% of this neighborhood while mechanistic cell-line studies dominate, leaving clinical screening approaches substantially underdeveloped.

entries.name: Canine mast cell tyrosine kinase
entries.one_line: Using tyrosine kinase inhibitor drugs to treat mast cell cancer in dogs.
entries.synthesis_md: c-kit mutations in cutaneous mast cell tumors drive tumor cell proliferation and survival, making the KIT receptor the central therapeutic target in canine oncology [PMID 26021891]. Masitinib and toceranib inhibit c-kit and related receptor tyrosine kinases, producing objective responses in mast cell tumors, anal sac adenocarcinoma, and thyroid carcinoma [PMID 27136424, PMID 22630170]. KIT expression extends beyond mast cell disease — CD117 appears in canine soft tissue sarcomas, opening TKI therapy to tumors typically limited to surgery [PMID 40271490]. Combining masitinib with vinblastine reduces effective drug doses dramatically in preclinical tumor cell line models [PMID 21333567]. Adding COX inhibitors to TKI therapy blocks a parallel survival pathway that enables resistance [PMID 29942116]. IHC-based receptor panels are now needed to match individual tumors to the right kinase inhibitor before treatment begins [PMID 39126006].
entries.gap_line: Breed-specific genetic predisposition is nearly absent from the literature (only 1% of papers) despite being clinically essential for screening and prevention strategies in canine populations.

entries.name: Canine cardiac hemangiosarcoma
entries.one_line: A type of cancer affecting blood vessels in dogs' hearts, often forming in the cardiac chambers.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine cardiac hemangiosarcoma is the most common primary heart tumor in dogs [PMID 17270204, 26919146]. Golden Retrievers are disproportionately affected, and the disease strikes predominantly older animals [PMID 23811814]. The right atrium is the classic location, but the tumor also arises at the atrioventricular junction and left atrial base, where infiltration triggers arrhythmias and conduction failure. Hemorrhagic pericardial effusion leading to cardiac tamponade defines the acute crisis [PMID 40443234]. Concurrent splenic and cardiac disease is an established pattern: among dogs presenting with splenic HSA, 8.7% carried a simultaneous cardiac mass, and all were Golden Retrievers [PMID 21852509]. Surgical excision with pericardial free-patch repair is feasible for right auricular lesions [PMID 21498592]. Palliative radiation decreases tamponade risk, but no protocol has yet established a clear survival benefit [PMID 40443234].
entries.gap_line: Genetic basis for breed predisposition is unexplored despite well-documented hereditary patterns in susceptible breeds like German Shepherds and Golden Retrievers.

entries.name: Canine congenital cardiac defects
entries.one_line: Heart defects dogs are born with, affecting how the chambers and valves work.
entries.synthesis_md: Rare congenital cardiac malformations in dogs span a wide structural spectrum, including cor triatriatum dexter, double outlet right ventricle, double outlet right atrium, double-chambered right ventricle, unroofed coronary sinus, and auricular aneurysm [PMID 35366638, PMID 32980747, PMID 38851121, PMID 31174725]. Affected animals typically present in the first months to years of life with tachypnea, exercise intolerance, cyanosis, or ascites. Transthoracic echocardiography is the first-line diagnostic tool, but ECG-gated computed tomography angiography resolves anatomic ambiguity — particularly for lesions such as unroofed coronary sinus, where the septal defect is absent yet abnormal inter-atrial flow persists [PMID 41096429, PMID 37269624]. Interventional cardiology offers durable palliation: balloon dilation of the obstructive membrane in cor triatriatum dexter relieves right-sided congestive heart failure, and stenting sustains that relief when stenosis recurs [PMID 35320744, PMID 34303120]. Open-heart surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass successfully corrects lesions not amenable to catheter-based repair.
entries.gap_line: Breeding programs and genetic prevention of inherited defects are nearly absent from research, accounting for only 2% of the literature.

entries.name: Canine tumor epidemiology breed
entries.one_line: Research mapping cancer types and risk factors across dog breeds and ages.
entries.synthesis_md: Hemangiosarcoma accounts for roughly 2.80% of all canine tumor diagnoses in large registry datasets, placing it consistently among the ten most common tumors across the dog's lifespan [PMID 27406312]. Breed is a primary driver of that risk: flat-coated retrievers carry an outsized malignant tumor burden, with 411 of 1,023 tumor submissions from that breed classified as malignant [PMID 11037728]. Swiss registry data spanning more than five decades confirm that age, body size, sex, and neuter status each independently shape which dogs develop tumors [PMID 27406312]. Multi-institutional networks like Vet-OncoNet and broad Australian population studies are now standardizing how these demographic risk factors are captured across pathology laboratories and clinical practices [PMID 35202324, PMID 30307659]. Portuguese laboratory data reinforce that malignant tumors represent more than a third of all canine cutaneous submissions, underscoring the need for consistent national-level tracking [PMID 35216632]. Hemangiosarcoma risk clusters predictably by breed and demographics — a signal that cancer surveillance infrastructure is only beginning to quantify at scale.
entries.gap_line: Early detection and intervention strategies are vastly underdeveloped relative to breed-risk characterization.

entries.name: Canine hemangiosarcoma clinical management
entries.one_line: Surgical and medical management of hemangiosarcoma, a rapidly fatal cancer in dogs.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine hemangiosarcoma (HSA) arises from vascular endothelial cells and can originate anywhere in the body, but the spleen, liver, and heart are the dominant primary sites. Most affected dogs are older than eight years, and more than half present with sudden collapse caused by tumor rupture and internal hemorrhage. Breed predispositions are well established, and a case-control study of the Lazio regional tumor registry identified both intrinsic and extrinsic risk factors for visceral HSA [PMID 32634752]. Whole-body CT reveals skeletal muscle metastases more often than clinical signs alone would predict, underscoring the disease's systemic reach at diagnosis. Rare primary locations—including the falciform ligament and intrapelvic musculature—carry distinct presentations but share the same aggressive biology. Surgery plus adjuvant chemotherapy extends survival beyond the 86-day median seen after surgery alone, and fenofibrate has shown additional survival benefit as an adjunct agent. Despite multimodal treatment, HSA remains largely incurable in most clinical settings.
entries.gap_line: Molecular mechanisms underlying breed predisposition lack characterization despite breed risk being epidemiologically well-documented.

entries.name: Canine splenic hemangiosarcoma
entries.one_line: Hemangiosarcoma is a deadly blood vessel cancer in dogs' spleens that researchers study to improve surgical outcomes.
entries.synthesis_md: The "double two-thirds rule" — that two-thirds of splenic masses are malignant and two-thirds of malignant splenic tumors are hemangiosarcoma — overstates cancer risk in clinical practice. A prospective cohort of 345 dogs found benign lesions in 35.7% of cases, and a 182-case retrospective series placed benign diagnoses at 57.7%, with hemangiosarcoma confirmed in only 32.4%. The validated HeLP score, built from 406 dogs across five academic centers, gives clinicians a data-driven preoperative risk estimate. Routine complete blood count ratios — neutrophil-to-lymphocyte and platelet-to-lymphocyte — carry independent diagnostic and prognostic value in splenic hemangiosarcoma. Reliable tissue diagnosis requires multiple sections per specimen; Monte Carlo modeling of 413 sections across 50 cases confirms that single-section sampling misses hemangiosarcoma in a meaningful fraction of cases. Liver hemangiosarcoma carries its own survival profile distinct from splenic disease [PMID 36355453], and ectopic splenic tissue discovered after splenectomy can complicate post-surgical staging [PMID 41039975].
entries.gap_line: Molecular mechanisms of hemangiosarcoma initiation and angiogenesis progression lack exploration, with literature dominated by clinical detection and surgical treatment.

entries.name: Canine tumor immune checkpoint expression
entries.one_line: Research on immune checkpoints in canine cancers and how they control tumor immunity.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine hemangiosarcoma shows constitutive NF-kB activation, confirmed by nuclear staining for p65 and p100/p52 subunits on immunohistochemistry [PMID 37357953]. The same signaling pattern appears across canine lymphomas, histiocytic sarcomas, and mast cell tumors, placing HSA within a broader immune-evasive canine cancer landscape [PMID 37357953]. Mismatch repair deficiency — established as a pan-tumor predictor of checkpoint immunotherapy response in humans regardless of tumor type — has now been characterized in canine neoplasms, creating a framework to evaluate checkpoint blockade eligibility across canine solid tumors [PMID 34538134]. In canine mammary tumors, PD-L1 expression is driven by the EGFR-STAT3 signaling axis and is suppressible by the natural compound isorhamnetin [PMID 38203840]. Canine oral melanoma studies show that T-cell infiltration and microenvironment composition differ by molecular subgroup, establishing that immune checkpoint landscapes are heterogeneous and subtype-dependent in canine solid tumors [PMID 35053440].
entries.gap_line: Clinical development of immune checkpoint inhibitors for canine tumors is nearly absent despite two decades of expression profiling, representing just 6% of papers.

entries.name: Canine lymphoma tumor suppressors
entries.one_line: Studies of which genes fail in canine lymphoma tumors to predict outcomes and understand the disease in people.
entries.synthesis_md: Research on canine lymphoma has revealed recurring disruptions in tumor suppressor pathways and epigenetic regulation. The p16/CDKN2A locus is frequently silenced by promoter methylation in canine lymphoma cells, allowing unrestrained retinoblastoma protein phosphorylation and cell-cycle progression [PMID 36006308]. Genome-wide methylation profiling in canine diffuse large B-cell lymphoma identified additional silenced genes—including HOXD10, FGFR2, and RASAL3—that are partially reactivated by hypomethylating agents such as decitabine [PMID 30533020], and doxorubicin-resistant DLBCL cells accumulate thousands of additional hypermethylated promoter regions compared to sensitive controls [PMID 33961622]. At the somatic mutation level, TP53 alteration correlates with elevated tumor mutational burden across multiple canine cancer types [PMID 34344882]. Gene expression profiling further distinguishes at least three major molecular lymphoma groups in dogs—low-grade T-cell, high-grade T-cell, and B-cell—each with distinct pathway activation [PMID 23125145], while canine follicular lymphoma mirrors its human counterpart through BCL2 dysregulation and marked genomic heterogeneity [PMID 27135201].
entries.gap_line: Therapeutic development targeting p53 and p16 loss in canine lymphoma cells is completely absent despite two decades of breed-screening genetic discovery.

entries.name: canine soft tissue sarcoma
entries.one_line: Studies of soft tissue cancers in dogs, examining tumor genetics, immune responses, and surgical treatment outcomes.
entries.synthesis_md: Achieving tumor-free surgical margins reduces local recurrence risk in canine cutaneous and subcutaneous soft tissue sarcoma, though the clinical significance of "clean but close" margins remains unresolved—a binary complete/incomplete classification fails to capture the full spectrum of recurrence risk [PMID 30953384]. Pathologist grading carries meaningful intra- and inter-observer variability, meaning the same tumor can receive different grades depending on who reads the slide [PMID 28133880]. Multi-institutional data on STS in the region of the ischiatic tuberosity show that outcomes mirror those at other anatomic sites, with grade and margin status—not location—driving prognosis [PMID 35420253]. Whole-transcriptome profiling reveals distinct gene expression patterns across STS subtypes despite their historically shared clinical management [PMID 36099287]. In histiocytic sarcoma—a particularly aggressive STS subtype—circulating lymphocytes upregulate PD-1 and CTLA-4, identifying immune checkpoint blockade as a candidate therapeutic strategy [PMID 26901565].
entries.gap_line: Surgical and chemotherapy efficacy studies are underrepresented (6% of papers) compared to breed-risk genetics (32%), limiting clinical outcome knowledge.

entries.name: Canine genetic disease variants
entries.one_line: Identifying genetic variants that contribute to disease risk across dog breeds and populations.
entries.synthesis_md: Germline genetic variants—not just somatic mutations acquired in tumors—drive breed-specific cancer predisposition in dogs [PMID 26588071]. Breeds with limited genetic diversity, including those with known cancer susceptibility like the Leonberger, concentrate risk alleles at elevated frequencies throughout the breeding population [PMID 33054768]. Genome-wide association studies paired with whole-genome sequencing in case-control cohorts of dozens to a few hundred dogs reliably identify causal loci for complex breed diseases [PMID 40043055, PMID 32486318]. A single missense variant in one gene can be sufficient to confer disease risk across an entire breed [PMID 31095560]. A weighted SNP risk score built from GWAS hits failed to replicate in an independent validation cohort, illustrating the difficulty of dissecting complex inherited disease in numerically small breed populations [PMID 40043055]. Commercial genetic testing datasets now enable population-scale, longitudinal tracking of disease variant allele frequencies across breeds and geographies [PMID 38003037].
entries.gap_line: Treatment studies comprise only 2% of research, creating a massive gap between robust variant discovery and clinical therapeutic application.

entries.name: Canine genome breed variation
entries.one_line: How selective breeding created genetic diversity in dog breeds and what researchers learn from studying genetic variation.
entries.synthesis_md: Dog breeds carry extensive, breed-specific linkage disequilibrium across megabase-scale genomic intervals, making genome-wide association studies in closed purebred populations far more tractable than in outbred humans [PMID 15545498]. Resources like Dog10K and BarkBase catalog canine multi-omics data and epigenomic regulatory elements across tissues, providing the reference infrastructure needed to interpret disease-associated variants [PMID 39436034, 31181663]. Selective breeding that concentrated alleles for morphology and behavior also concentrated disease-risk alleles within breeds [PMID 27874836]. The same GWAS frameworks that identified X-linked loci controlling body size in large breeds [PMID 28257443] and structural variants linked to obesity risk in Labrador Retrievers [PMID 37799139] are directly applicable to mapping hemangiosarcoma susceptibility in high-risk breeds. This combination of within-breed uniformity and between-breed divergence makes the dog an unusually powerful platform for isolating the genetic contributors to breed-enriched cancers like HSA.
entries.gap_line: Canine disease prediction from genomic data is underexplored; early-detection studies comprise just 3% despite extensive mapping of breed-associated variants.

entries.name: Canine histiocytic sarcoma genetics
entries.one_line: Genetic studies of histiocytic sarcoma, a cancer that affects certain dog breeds.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine histiocytic sarcoma (HS) strikes Bernese Mountain Dogs and Flat-Coated Retrievers at rates far exceeding the general dog population, pointing to a heritable predisposition in these breeds [PMID 21615919, PMID 36136714]. Molecular cytogenetic profiling of affected breeds identifies recurrent DNA copy number aberrations, including deletions of tumor suppressor genes, as core genomic drivers [PMID 21615919]. Activating mutations in key signaling genes add a second layer of genetic disruption specifically in Bernese Mountain Dogs with HS [PMID 31277422]. Despite sharing high susceptibility, Bernese Mountain Dogs and Flat-Coated Retrievers differ in their clinical presentations, demonstrating that genetic background actively shapes tumor behavior [PMID 36136714]. In the bloodstream, circulating let-7g microRNA falls markedly in Bernese Mountain Dogs diagnosed with disseminated HS compared to healthy dogs, opening a path toward minimally invasive detection [PMID 26792388]. These converging genomic and clinical patterns mirror rare histiocytic malignancies in humans, establishing the dog as a tractable natural model for this cancer [PMID 21615919].
entries.gap_line: Therapeutic interventions remain almost entirely unexplored (2% of literature) despite extensive breed-risk mapping, leaving genetics findings without translational pathways to treatment.

entries.name: Canine mammary tumor models
entries.one_line: Dogs with mammary tumors provide insights into how breast cancer develops and progresses in humans.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine mammary tumors mirror human breast cancer across shared environmental risk factors, hormone receptor profiles (ER, PR, HER2), histological features, and conserved genomic architecture, making dogs a valid spontaneous model for translational oncology [PMID 38766503, PMID 29056711]. Researchers have built durable in vitro platforms from this biology: a living biobank of 24 organoid lines from 16 dogs enables functional drug testing in tissue that retains original tumor heterogeneity [PMID 36302863]. Earlier work established three cell lines (DE-E, DE-F, DE-SF) that survived more than 200 passages with measurable chemotherapy responses, providing a reproducible screening substrate [PMID 19748110]. Adjunct strategies are also under evaluation — ozone therapy combined with carboplatin in clinical canine patients affected oxidative profile and quality of life relative to carboplatin alone [PMID 38929112]. Genome-wide DNA methylation analysis, shown to stratify prognosis in canine lymphoma patients treated with CHOP, points toward epigenetic liquid-biopsy tools for mammary tumors as well [PMID 38115210].
entries.gap_line: Treatment efficacy studies are severely underrepresented at 4% while early detection and screening account for 46%, leaving therapeutic outcomes unexplored.

entries.name: VEGF canine tumor angiogenesis
entries.one_line: Blood vessel growth in dog tumors driven by a key protein, used to predict cancer outcomes.
entries.synthesis_md: Canine hemangiosarcoma originates from malignant vascular endothelial cells, and researchers have built a reliable marker toolkit to confirm that diagnosis at the bench. CD31 antigen marks every HSA case tested, while von Willebrand factor misses roughly a quarter of tumors—making CD31 the stronger endothelial identifier [PMID 7593754]. Factor VIII-related antigen stains 89% of HSAs, even in poorly differentiated lesions [PMID 3136585]. Claudin-5, an endothelium-specific tight-junction protein, is strongly expressed in HSA but absent from non-vascular spindle-cell tumors, sharpening differential diagnosis [PMID 19475526]. In blood, elevated serum big endothelin-1 detects splenic HSA with 100% sensitivity, opening a noninvasive diagnostic route [PMID 25920760]. Angiopoietins-1 and -2 regulate the new vessel growth that sustains these tumors across canine cancers [PMID 16545849], and blocking VEGFR2 slows tumor vasculature development in canine malignancies, pointing toward anti-angiogenic treatment strategies directly applicable to HSA [PMID 41319225].
entries.gap_line: Treatment approaches to VEGF-driven canine tumors are severely underdeveloped relative to early detection work, leaving therapeutic translation unexplored.

entries.name: Canine mast cell tumors
entries.one_line: Studies of mast cell cancer in dogs, examining tumor features that predict patient survival.
entries.synthesis_md: Mast cell tumors are the most common malignant skin tumors in dogs; KIT proto-oncogene exon 11 internal tandem duplications correlate strongly with high histologic grade but do not independently predict outcome beyond grade alone [PMID 35505524]. About 25% of cutaneous MCTs metastasize, and global gene expression profiling identifies molecular signatures that separate metastatic from non-metastatic primary tumors even when c-kit mutations are absent [PMID 30566430]. For histologic grade 2 cases, proliferation markers—Ki-67, PCNA, AgNOR, and survivin—resolve the behavioral uncertainty that grading cannot. High-grade disease spreads rapidly to intrathoracic sites, including the pericardium, producing life-threatening effusion [PMID 33222871]. Immune checkpoint proteins PD-L1 and CTLA-4, along with the RANK/RANK-L signaling axis, mark both primary tumors and lymph node metastases, identifying actionable immunotherapy targets [PMID 37370399]. Elevated plasma 2-arachidonoylglycerol in affected dogs compared to healthy controls places the endocannabinoid system inside this tumor's molecular landscape.
entries.gap_line: Randomized treatment trials are absent, leaving a field of 111 papers dominated by retrospective tumor classification and prognostic studies.

entries.name: Canine spinal cord tumors
entries.one_line: Studies of spinal cord tumors in dogs, focusing on diagnostic imaging and disease characteristics.
entries.synthesis_md: Hemangiosarcoma is the most common metastatic tumor involving the canine brain, yet detailed MRI descriptions of how it looks in the central nervous system were lacking until a seven-institution retrospective study characterized its imaging features across histopathologically confirmed cases affecting both the brain and vertebral column [PMID 34873768]. That MRI gap matters because clinicians diagnosing CNS signs in dogs must work through a broad differential: meningiomas are the well-characterized primary intracranial tumors in older dogs, typically appearing as dura-adherent masses on the dorsal brain surface [PMID 3750730], while rarer entities—granular cell tumors of lumbar spinal nerves [PMID 20622241] and unusual intradural spinal masses in young dogs [PMID 17523097]—add further complexity. Against that landscape, clear MRI criteria for hemangiosarcoma help clinicians distinguish metastatic vascular disease from primary tumors, which demand entirely different management.
entries.gap_line: Systematic treatment outcome data for chemotherapy and radiation therapy are missing despite 6% treatment papers, contrasting with 48% on breed screening.

entries.name: Canine hemangiosarcoma clinical cases
entries.one_line: Clinical presentations and treatment outcomes of hemangiosarcoma, a vascular cancer, in dogs.
entries.synthesis_md: Hemangiosarcoma strikes dogs at a wider range of anatomical sites than the classic spleen-heart-skin triad. Primary tumors arise in the urinary bladder, perianal dermis, and cervical lymph nodes as well as the spleen and heart. Bladder disease can be resolved by partial cystectomy alone [PMID 32412336]. Cervical nodal primaries often present as slow-growing, grade 1–2 masses with no systemic signs, and surgical excision achieved complete removal in all four documented cases [PMID 27767434]. Splenic tumors spread via lymphatics to the liver, heart, and regional nodes, establishing the spleen as the dominant metastatic source in multi-organ disease. Cardiac involvement carries the highest acute risk: atrial tumors can breach the myocardial wall and trigger fatal tamponade [PMID 38583979]. Immunohistochemistry is essential for distinguishing hemangiosarcoma from related vascular malignancies such as intestinal lymphangiosarcoma [PMID 38141386].
entries.gap_line: Therapeutic trials comparing treatment approaches are absent from a literature dominated by breed screening and diagnostic case reporting.
